-  MOS 1710, Offensive Cyberspace Warfare Officer (III) (CWO5 to WO) PMOS,
## DC I
a.  Summary.  The Offensive Cyberspace Warfare Officer is a SME in
offensive cyberspace operations (OCO).  They are technical experts
experienced in clandestine and overt offensive cyberspace warfare, weapons
platform design, infrastructure utilization, and the use of intelligence
products and data to support planning and execution of OCO.  These Marines
possess an advanced understanding of the doctrines, policies, and authorities
that govern maneuver warfare, the application of force in and through
cyberspace, and intelligence procedures.  As principal technical advisors on
all aspects of OCO, they leverage this knowledge to provide recommendations
and advice to commanders to understand second and third-order effects in
cyberspace, drive target pairing, conduct weaponeering, initiate cascading
effects into the physical domains, and satisfy collection requirements in
cyberspace.  Marines in this MOS may be assigned to a BIC (within their
occupational field) that has been coded with one or more DoD cyber workforce
framework (DCWF) work role codes.  Please reference DoDI 8140.10, DoDM
8140.10, and the MOS roadmap for additional training and certification
requirements associated with these work role codes.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be willing to submit to a counterintelligence scope
polygraph examination.
(2) Security requirement:
(a) Must complete SCI pre-screen interview with HQMC SSO for
assignment qualification per DoN and HQMC SCI policies and procedures.
(b) Must have a current single scope background investigation/T5
investigation and adjudicated for sensitive compartmented information (SCI)
eligibility.
(3) Must meet eligibility and appointment criteria as prescribed in
MCO 1040.42B, WO and LDO accession programs.

## 1114 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-90 Enclosure (1)
(4) Must have completed the Joint Cyber Analysis Course (JCAC)
(N23CUQ1), Center for Naval Cryptology, Corry Station, NAS Pensacola, FL.
(5) WOs assigned this MOS must have previously served in one of the
following related MOSs for a minimum of three year 1712, 1713, 1721, or 2611.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Warrant Officer Basic Course (WOBC) (M02RMN4) within
18 months of appointment to WO.
(2) Marines serving in billets coded with an additional skill
designator (ASD), as per the table of organization, must attain the
appropriate certification level within 24 months of billet assignment.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) Mid-level Management Marines (CWO3 to WO) holding mid-level

(a) Computer and Information Systems Manager 11-3021.

(a) Computer and Information Research Scientists 15-1221.

## 1019.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Operational Advisor, 0571.
## (2) Cyberspace Warfare Operator, 1721.
-  MOS 1720, Defensive Cyberspace Weapons Officer (III) (CWO5 to WO) PMOS,
## DC I
a.  Summary.  The Defensive Cyberspace Weapons Officer is a subject
matter expert (SME) in defensive cyberspace operations (DCO) with advanced
and mastery knowledge of defensive cyber weapons systems and platforms and
their employment.  They serve as master defensive operators, technical

## 1114 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-91 Enclosure (1)
directors, top-level representatives, special staff officers, and principal
technical advisors to selected headquarters and staffs.  1720s coordinate and
synchronize all defensive cyberspace missions and related activities with
representatives of other information-related capabilities in peer, higher,
and adjacent staff organizations and advise commands conducting, supporting,
or assisting in providing critical input and analysis in the development,
review, and update of OPLANS, CONPLANS, cyberspace operations annexes to
operations, operational orders, training, exercises, and letters of
instruction.  In doing such, Defensive Cyberspace Weapons Officers improve
the commanders decision-making during planning and execution by integrating
DCO capabilities into the development of problem framing and desired end
states by identifying friendly and enemy cyber vulnerabilities, challenging
assumptions about cyberspace, offering alternative perspectives, and aiding
in the development of best courses of action at the tactical and operational
levels.  Most notably, 1720s utilize knowledge and experience in order to
outmaneuver adversaries in cyberspace, defend operational missions and
associated key terrain in cyberspace, and employ effects to ensure friendly
freedom of movement in and through cyberspace to leverage capabilities,
maximize effects, and optimize combat effectiveness in operational domains
and across warfighting functions.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a U.S. citizen.
(2) Must be willing to submit to a counterintelligence scope
polygraph examination.
(3) Possess one of the following aptitude test scores:
(a) A cyber test score of 60 or higher.
(b) A combined math and verbal score of 1000 or higher on the
Scholastic Aptitude Test (SAT).
(c) An armed services vocational aptitude battery (ASVAB)
electrical composite (EL) standard score of 110 or higher and general
technical composite (GT) standard score of 110 or higher.
(d) A combined math and English score of 39 or higher on the
American College Test (ACT).  Applicants submitting SAT or ACT test scores
will submit a certified copy of the test report.
(4) Be a high school graduate or possess the service-accepted
equivalent as prescribed by current directives.
(5) No convictions by court-martial, civilian courts or non-judicial
punishment for any cyber/information technology-related offense within the
last three years.
(6) Must have no record of derogatory information or unfavorable
conduct that cast doubt on the Marine’s trustworthiness and honesty.
(7) Must have previously held MOSs 0631, 0639, 0671, 0679, 0699,
1711, 1721, 1799, 2611, 2651, 2652, or 2659.  This prerequisite may be waived
for extremely qualified applicants from other occupation fields on a case-by-
case basis by HQMC, DC I, Information Maneuver Division.

## 1114 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-92 Enclosure (1)
(8) Security requirement:  must meet sensitive compartmented
information (SCI) eligibility guidelines and complete SCI pre-screen
interview with HQMC SSO for assignment qualification per DoN and HQMC SCI
policies and procedures based on a single scope background T5 investigation
prior to starting the Warrant Officer Basic Course (WOBC) (M02RMN4), The
Basic School, Quantico, VA.
(9) Evidence of prior achievement as indicated in the following order
of desirability:
(a) A strong background in cyberspace operations with evidence of
having completed joint qualification requirements for a host or network
analyst.
(b) Assigned or serving in MOS 1721 for a minimum of 24 months
under the purview of, or within, U.S. Cyber Command (e.g. Cyber Mission Force
(CMF), Cyber National Mission Force (CNMF), DCO-IDM Companies, or Marine
## Forces Cyberspace Command).
(c) Endorsed by a Defensive Cyberspace Weapons Officer (1720);
## Offensive Cyberspace Warfare Officer (1710); Cyberspace Warfare Development
Officer, (1705); Cyberspace Warfare Officer (1702); or Cyberspace Warfare
Chief (1799) stating that the individual meets the minimum technical
expertise requirements for a Defensive Cyberspace Weapons Officer.
(d) Though desirable attributes, a college degree and
certifications do not replace operational experience.
c.  Requirements
(1) Defensive Cyberspace Weapons Officers must have an advanced
comprehensive and theoretical knowledge of:
(a) Digital forensics.
(b) Malware analysis.
(c) Classified national systems.
(d) Cyberspace policy and doctrine.
(e) Cyber acquisition management.
(f) Defensive cyberspace operations.
(g) Cyber-related legal considerations.
(h) Cyberspace command and control.
(i) Use of information and data to conduct cyber analytics.
(j) The cyberspace environment and environment monitoring.
(k) The purpose and proper employment of military intelligence.
(l) The joint operational planning process and the Marine Corps
planning process.

## 1114 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-93 Enclosure (1)
(m) Plans, concepts, strategies, and tactics for development and
employment of defensive cyberspace weapons systems.
(n) Facility-related control systems (FRCS)/Industrial Control
Systems (ICS)/Platform Information Technology (PIT)/and Supervisory Control,
and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems.
(2) Mastery knowledge and demonstrated master-level proficiency of
the following core competencies, which must be trained to, exercised, and
sustained:
(a) Cyberspace hunt operations.
(b) Internal Defensive Measure (IDM) operations.
(c) Cyberspace Incident Response (IR) operations.
(d) Cyberspace Threat Emulation (CTE) operations.
(3) Must complete the Warrant Officer Basic Course (M02RMN4) within
18 months of appointment to Warrant Officer.
(4) Must successfully complete the first available Cyber Operations
Planning Course (COPC) (A09BN72) upon graduation from the Warrant Officer
Basic Course (WOBC) (M02RMN4), The Basic School, Quantico, VA.
(5) Must obtain and maintain the appropriate IAT Level III and IAM
Level III certifications in accordance with DOD 8570.01-m.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) Computer and Information Systems Managers 11-3021.
## (2) Information Security Analysts 15-1212.
(3) Computer and Information Research Scientists 15-1220.
## (4) Data Scientists 15-2051.
## (5) Computer Hardware Engineers 17-2061.
(6) Command and Control Center Officers 55-1015.
(7) Military Officer Special and Tactical Operations Leaders/
## Managers, All Other 55-1019.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Cyberspace Warfare Officer, 1702.
## (2) Cyberspace Warfare Development Officer, 1705.
## (3) Cyberspace Warfare Operator, 1721.
## (4) Cyberspace Warfare Chief, 1799.

## 1114 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-94 Enclosure (1)
-  MOS 1730, Civil Affairs Officer (Gen to 2ndLt) (I) FMOS, DC I
a.  Summary.  Civil Affairs Officers assist the MAGTF commander to plan,
coordinate, and conduct civil affairs operations and civil-military
operations.  They help ensure the civil implications of all MAGTF missions
are considered throughout the Marine Corps Planning Process.  Civil Affairs
Officers act as an interface between the MAGTF commander and a wide variety
of civilian populations, agencies, and organizations.  CA Officers informs,
influence, shape, and gain access to the cognitive civil environment through
targeted and integrated civil reconnaissance and civil engagements.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Security requirement:  secret security clearance eligibility.
(2) Must be a major, captain, and 1st or 2nd lieutenant of any MOS.
Majors with more than four years’ time-in-grade are ineligible for initial
civil affairs qualification training.
c.  Requirements.  Complete the Civil Affairs Officer Course (M020A3D)
conducted at MCCMOS, MCB Quantico, VA.
d.  Duties
(1) Develop civil affairs plans (engagements, assessments, civil
reconnaissance, etc.) and advise the commander on the conduct of civil
affairs operations (CA activities, military government operations, CA
supported activities) throughout the conflict continuum for all operations,
contingencies, and exercises.
(2) Integrate civil affairs activities and tasks (engagements,
assessments, civil reconnaissance, etc.) with supported unit.
(3) Provide civil preparation of the battlespace (civil
considerations) into the intelligence preparation of the battlespace and
marine corps planning process.
(4) Participate in the CMO working group, information operations
working group, targeting board and other B2C2WGs as appropriate.
(5) Coordinate and liaise with interagency, host nation,
international and non-governmental personnel.
(6) Establish relationships to shape, inform, influence, and exploit
within the battlespace and synchronize efforts with information related
capabilities and other elements of the MAGTF to leverage capabilities and
maximize effects.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Emergency Management Specialist 13-1061.

f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Advisor, 0570.
## (2) Operational Advisor, 0571.

## 1114 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-95 Enclosure (1)
## (3) Influence Officer, 1707.
-  MOS 1760, Maritime Space Operations Mission Commander (I) (LtCol to
2ndLt) NMOS (0202, 0602, 1702, 1706), DC I
a.  Summary.  Maritime Space Operations Mission Commanders are Marine
officers who have acquired introductory level training in C5ISRT systems and
methods for countering these systems.  They serve as mission commanders for
one or more tactical-level teams.  They plan, direct, train, and facilitate
the deployment and employment of one or more teams.  To fulfill these
responsibilities, they evaluate intelligence, estimate the operational
situation, and formulate, coordinate, and execute appropriate plans for the
use, employment, communication, operational logistics and maintenance of
associated information warfare technology.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a U.S. citizen.
(2) Security requirement:  Must have a current T5 investigation and
adjudicated for SCI eligibility.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Army Space Control Planners Course (ASCP) (F25M074),
USA SMDCoE, Colorado Springs, CO.
(2) Following completion of ASCP, complete either Marine Corps
Systems Command new equipment training program or Training Command Space
Capabilities and Mission Planning Course. MOS 1760 is assigned upon approval
of the Information Maneuver Occupational Field Manager.
(3) This MOS may be awarded to Marines that previously completed the
listed training.
(4) Any of these requirements may be waived by the Deputy Commandant
for Information based on educational or work experience.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Command and Control Center Officer 55-1015.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Maritime Space Officer, 1706.
## (2) System Operator, 1762.
## (3) Radio Frequency Operator, 1766.
-  MOS 1781, Basic Information Planner (I) (LtCol to Capt) FMOS, DC I
a.  Summary.  Formerly known as the 0510 FMOS.  Information Operations
Practitioners are focused on the information environment (IE), operational
planning and coordination, IE characterization/analysis, and integration of
operations.  Duties encompass the supervision of tasking, processing, and

## 1114 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-96 Enclosure (1)
dissemination of products related to information advantage; operation
security (OPSEC), military deception (MILDEC), cyberspace operations,
electronic warfare (EW), special effects, communications strategy and
operations (COMMSTRAT), counterintelligence (CI), civil military operations
(CMO), and military information support operations (MISO).  Basic Information
Planners prepare and issue reports to include target audience analysis
reports, technical reports, and summaries.
b.  Prerequisites.  Security requirement:  active secret security
clearance.
c.  Requirements.  Complete the MAGTF Operations in the Information
Environment Practitioner Course (MOPC) (M02F2GD), Quantico, VA.
d.  Duties
(1) Develops Information plans and annexes to operations plans for
all operations, contingencies and exercises.
(2) Coordinates with representatives of other Information forces on
the MAGTF staff.
(3) Acts as a liaison for Information forces that are not organic to
the MAGTF troop list.
(4) Advises the commanding officer and operations officer on the
conduct of information in order to provide continuity of objectives in the
multi-domain space.
(5) Provides subject matter expertise to the development of USMC
policy on information consistent with DoD policy.
(6) Provides USMC input to DoD information policy development.
(7) Assists in the development of USMC doctrine on information
consistent with USMC policy on IO.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Management Analysts 13-1111.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 1786, Space Operations Staff Officer (I) (LtCol to 2ndLt) FMOS, DC I
a.  Summary.  Space Operations Staff Officers are Marine officers who
have acquired introductory level training in the space environment, space-
based and space-enabled capabilities, and their applications to specific
warfighting capabilities.  They are able to integrate this knowledge into
training, operational planning, and execution in order to maximize
exploitation of these capabilities for the benefit of the commander and the
warfighter.
b.  Prerequisites.  Security requirement:  secret security clearance, SCI
eligible.
c.  Requirements.  Complete the Basic Space Operations Course (Space 200)
(F25M013) at the Peterson AFB, CO., or the Army Space Cadre Basic Course

## 1114 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-97 Enclosure (1)
(F25M054), US Army Space and Missile Defense Center of Excellence (USA
SMDCoE), Colorado Springs, CO.
d.  Duties
(1) Understand and apply space operations fundamentals, doctrine,
mission areas, and organizations.
(2) Understand basic impacts of and assist in planning the
operational space mission areas per Joint Publication 3-14, Joint Space
Operations.  Considerations will include employment of intelligence,
surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR); missile warning; environmental
monitoring; satellite communications (SATCOM); position, navigation, and
timing (PNT); and offensive and defensive space operations, to include
navigation warfare (NAVWAR).
(3) Provide space operations insight across all military occupational
specialties in order to support overall space domain integration within the
## MAGTF.
(4) Inform boards, bureaus, centers, cells, and working groups
(B2C2WG) on space operations considerations.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## None.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Maritime Space Officer, 1706.
## (2) Space Operations Officer, 8866.

## 1115 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-98 Enclosure (1)
## 1115.  OCCUPATIONAL FIELD 18, TANK, ASSAULT AMPHIBIOUS VEHICLE AND AMPHIBIOUS
## COMBAT VEHICLE
-  MOS 1802, Tank Officer (I) (LtCol to 2ndLt) PMOS, DC PP&O
a.  Summary.  THIS IS AN OBSOLETE MOS, NO LONGER IN USE IN THE TABLE OF
## ORGANIZATION OF THE MARINE CORPS.  THIS MOS IS MAINTAINED IN THE MOS MANUAL
## BECAUSE IT APPEARS IN THE RECORDS OF MARINES STILL ON ACTIVE DUTY OR THE
RESERVES.  Tank Officers command or assist in commanding tank units.  They
provide recommendations to the supported unit commander for the tactical
employment of tank units.  Tank Officers, as maneuver unit leaders, must be
able to evaluate intelligence and the operational situation.  Additionally,
they formulate, coordinate, and execute operation orders.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must have normal color vision.
(2) Must have a WS-B water survival.
(3) Must have vision correctable to 20/20.
(4) Must meet MOS classification standards, as listed in the glossary
(pg xiv) prior to graduating from the Basic School.
c.  Requirements.  Complete the Armor Officer Basic Course (A03T612) and
the Marine Annex to Armor Officer Basic Course (A03T5R2), Ft Moore, GA.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (m), Tank Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Armored Assault Vehicle Officers 55-1013.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 1803, Assault Amphibian (AA) Officer (I) (LtCol to 2ndLt) PMOS, DC
## PP&O
a.  Summary.  Assault Amphibian Officers command, or assist in
commanding, assault amphibian (AA) units, provide recommendations to the
supported unit commander for the tactical employment of AA units.  They also
direct AA units on maneuvers, tactical problems, and in combat.  In
conjunction with the U.S. Navy units, they control the ship to shore movement
of AAVs.  AAV Officers are responsible for the AA unit's personnel and
equipment readiness, operational employment, and the identification and
coordination of required logistics support.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must have normal color vision.
(2) Must have WS-I water survival qualification.
(3) Must have visual acuity correctable to 20/20 in both eyes.
(4) Must meet MOS classification standards, as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from the Basic School.

## 1115 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-99 Enclosure (1)
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Amphibious Combat Vehicle Platoon Commander Course
(M10AH53), Camp Pendleton, CA.
(2) Must possess the psychological and physiological qualifications
required for licensing as an Ordnance Vehicle Operator (see reference (cz)).
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (n), Assault Amphibious Vehicle Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Armored Assault Vehicle Officers 55-1013.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

## 1116 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-100 Enclosure (1)
## 1116.  OCCUPATIONAL FIELD 21, GROUND ORDNANCE MAINTENANCE
-  MOS 2102, Ordnance Officer (II) (LtCol to Capt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Ordnance Officers manage ordnance operations in the Marine
Forces, MEFs, bases, posts, and stations to include HQMC.  They serve as the
Ground Ordnance Maintenance Technical Advisor in general staff.  They may
command or assist in commanding a ground ordnance maintenance unit, or test,
evaluate, and advise in a foreign or joint environment.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must have previously held WO MOSs 2110, 2120, or 2125.
(2) Must meet eligibility and appointment criteria as prescribed in
MCO 1040.42B, WO and LDO accession programs.
c.  Requirements.  Achieve selection by the board to limited duty officer
## (LDO).
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Installation, Maintenance and Repair Occupations 49-0000.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 2110, Ordnance Vehicle Maintenance Officer (III) (CWO5 to WO) PMOS,
## DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Ordnance Vehicle Maintenance Officers manage the equipment
readiness, work progress, and maintenance personnel qualification/training.
They manage the activities of personnel engaged in the recovery, salvage, and
maintenance of weapon systems (ordnance vehicles).  They supervise the
functional areas of maintenance management, establish field maintenance
sites, and procedures for the defense of those sites.  They manage command
environmental hazardous waste programs, and supervise and provide oversight
of disposal, storage, emergency planning for hazardous waste, and ozone
depleting substances.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) This MOS may only be assigned to WOs previously qualified in MOSs
2141, 2146, 2147, or 2149.
(2) Must meet eligibility and appointment criteria as prescribed in
MCO 1040.42B, WO and LDO accession programs.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Ground Ordnance Maintenance Officer Course (A14RGZ6)
at U.S. Army Ordnance Center and School, Ft Gregg-Adams, VA.
(2) Complete the Warrant Officer Basic Course (WOBC) (M02RMN4), The
Basic School, Quantico, VA within 18 months of appointment to WO.

## 1116 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-101 Enclosure (1)
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers 49-
## 1011.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 2120, Ground Ordnance Maintenance Officer (III) (CWO5 to WO) PMOS, DC
## I&L
a.  Summary.  Ground Ordnance Maintenance Officers are special staff
officers who manage ground ordnance equipment readiness, accountability and
security. They serve as technical advisers on the appropriate employment of
ground ordnance maintenance resources. They oversee the activities of
personnel engaged in the recovery, salvage, and maintenance of weapons, fire
control systems, artillery, fires platforms, optical and electro-optical/
laser devices, small missile and other manned, remote, or semi-autonomous
ground ordnance systems.  They nest maintenance support packages into their
commander's mission requirements to enable mission accomplishment.  They must
possess skills in managing resources with an emphasis on efficient personnel
employment, maintaining equipment readiness, and exercise/deployment
planning.  Ground Ordnance Maintenance Officers must possess a working
knowledge of forecasting equipment readiness and availability, monitoring and
managing maintenance requirements, and effectively advising commanders. They
mentor the officers and Marines within their unit in all applicable physical,
doctrinal, and conceptual maintenance operations to improve the effectiveness
of the command.  They also develop maintenance policy at various levels, and
manage work progress and maintenance personnel qualification/training.  They
establish maintenance production lines, procedures for inspection, and
quality assurance on repaired equipment.  They supervise the functional areas
of maintenance management and can supervise the storage, disposal, and
emergency planning for hazardous waste and ozone depleting substances.  They
liaise with project managers and sustainment partners to address maintenance
requirements and to advance enterprise ingenuity in expeditionary ground
ordnance maintenance support and weapon system lethality.  Additional duties
will include participation in applicable course curriculum review boards
(CCRB), curriculum development for new equipment, and review of draft ground
ordnance maintenance training and readiness manuals.  Warrant Officers and
junior chief warrant officers generally serve in maintenance companies and
battalion staffs. They progress to serve on regimental staffs within the
ground combat element and within the command element and supporting
establishment.  At the most senior ranks, 2120s serve billets which provide
the greatest breadth of influence and include Headquarters Marine Corps
supporting activities that analyze, design, and develop standards-based
training for individual and collective progression, as well as billets which
support the requirements development and acquisition processes of ground
ordnance systems and related maintenance support equipment.  At this level,
Ground Ordnance Maintenance Officers must also be able to supervise
operational level maintenance, integrate maintenance support into major
subordinate command, service, and joint operations, manage maintenance policy
and doctrine, manage the training and education continuum for ground ordnance
maintenance personnel and provide staff level planning and technical advice
concerning maintenance capabilities and supportability for MAGTF commanders.


## 1116 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-102 Enclosure (1)
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must have previously served in one of the following MOSs 2111,
2112, 2131, 2161, 2171, or 2181.
(2) Must meet eligibility and appointment criteria as prescribed in
MCO 1040.42B, WO and LDO accession programs.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Ground Ordnance Maintenance Officer Course (A14RGZ6)
at U.S. Army Ordnance Center and School, Ft Gregg-Adams, VA.
(2) Complete the Warrant Officer Basic Course (WOBC) (M02RMN4), The
Basic School, Quantico, VA within 18 months of appointment to WO.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers 49-
## 1011.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Ordnance Officer, 2102.
## (2) Ordnance Vehicle Maintenance Officer, 2110.

## 1117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-103 Enclosure (1)
## 1117.  OCCUPATIONAL FIELD 23, AMMUNITION AND EXPLOSIVE ORDNANCE DISPOSAL
-  MOS 2305, Explosive Ordnance Disposal Officer (II/III) (LtCol to Capt)
and (CWO5 to WO) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD) Officers supervise
command and control EOD operations and personnel.  Additionally, they also
serve as special staff officers.  EOD Officers are responsible for the EOD
function in support of the MAGTF, supporting establishments, homeland
defense, and special operations forces by locating, accessing, identifying,
rendering safe, neutralizing, and disposing of hazards, both foreign and
domestic chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear, and high-yield
explosive (CBRNE), unexploded explosive ordnance (UXO), improvised explosive
device (IED), and weapons of mass destruction (WMD) that present a threat to
operations, installations, personnel, or materiel.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Security requirement:  top secret security clearance eligibility.
(2) Must have normal color vision and no claustrophobic tendencies.
(3) Only MOS 2305 WOs may apply for appointment as a MOS 2305 LDO.
(4) Must meet eligibility and appointment criteria as prescribed in
MCO 1040.42B, WO and LDO accession programs.
(5) Must meet the medical requirements listed in article 15-107 per
reference (ai) for explosive handling and/or explosive driver.
(6) This MOS may only be assigned to WOs who served at least five
years in the MOS 2336 having achieved, at a minimum, the Senior EOD Breast
Insignia Qualification.  This will be documented in a page 11 entry, and/or
through MCTFS, and should also be documented within the command endorsement
of the WO package and a letter of recommendation from a Marine Corps EOD
Officer serving in an EOD Officer billet.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Explosive Ordnance Disposal Officer Course
(M09B0E1), Twentynine Palms, CA.
(2) Warrant Officers must complete the Warrant Officer Basic Course
(WOBC) (M02RMN4) within 18 months of appointment.
(3) Requirements for continued qualification and progression training
all EOD officers will be annually screened for continued service in the EOD
MOS using NAVMC form 11362.  Based on the comprehensive standards covered
under NAVMC form 11362, EOD Officers will not be screened using NAVMC form
## 11386.
(4) The following core competencies must be trained to and sustained
by all EOD Officers.
(a) UXO operations.
(b) WMD operations.

## 1117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-104 Enclosure (1)
(c) Advanced electronics.
(d) IED defeat operations.
(e) Post blast investigation.
(f) Disassembly and inerting.
(g) Explosive ordnance exploitation.
(h) Specialized demolition techniques.
(i) Destruction of explosive ordnance.
(j) Explosive ordnance reconnaissance.
(k) EOD robotics employment and usage.
(l) Render safe of explosive ordnance (EO).
(m) Advanced TTPs for EOD tools and equipment.
(n) Homemade explosives (HME) identification and neutralization.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (by), Explosive Ordnance Disposal Technician/Officer Training and
Readiness Manual, and reference (ba), Marine Corps EOD Program.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Emergency Management Specialists 13-1061.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 2339, Marine EOD Diver Officer (II/III) (LtCol to Capt) and (CWO5 to
## WO) NMOS (2305), DC I&L
a.  Summary.  A Marine EOD Officer who is trained to dive and use
littoral explosive ordnance neutralization (LEON) equipment in the underwater
environment.  This title and MOS will also be used to identify Marine
Explosive Ordnance Disposal (EOD) Diver Officer Billets in the T/Os.
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be a graduate of Naval School Explosive Ordnance
Disposal (NAVSCOLEOD) (N56GPX1).
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the MK 16 Underwater Breathing Apparatus (UBA) Diver
Course (N20YC11) at the Naval Dive and Salvage Training Center, Panama City,
## FL.
(2) Complete the Navy Scuba Diver Course (N2003E1) or the Navy Basic
Scuba Diver Course (A-433-0123) at the Naval Dive and Salvage Training
Center, Panama City, FL.
(3) Complete the EOD Basic Navy Underwater Course (N56B0Q1) at
NAVSCOLEOD, Eglin AFB Florida or must complete the LEON MOJT program
supervised by a Marine EOD Diver (MOS 2339).

## 1117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-105 Enclosure (1)
(4) Must maintain physically qualified per naval special warfare/
special operations physical to perform diver duties in accordance with inter-
service requirements established in NAVMED P-117 and reference, standards of
medical fitness.
d.  Duties.  (LtCol to Capt) and (CWO5 to WO):  Plans, leads and employs
Marine EOD LEON equipment; conducting underwater operations to detect,
locate, reacquire, render safe/neutralize, recover, exploit, and dispose of
underwater explosive ordnance in the very shallow water and surf zone.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Commercial Diver 49-9092.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 2340, Ammunition Officer (II/III) (LtCol to Capt) and (CWO5 to WO)
## PMOS, MCSC
a.  Summary.  Ammunition Officers supervise and coordinate ammunition
supply and renovation functions.  These functions include, but are not
limited to, procurement, receipt, storage, issue, handling, shipment,
salvage, and renovation of ammunition.  They also operate ammunition supply
points and are responsible for the establishment of field ammunition supply
points in forward areas.  Ammunition Officers are assigned to billets on the
general staff level and are responsible for advising those commanders on
matters relating to the development of ammunition allowances for both
training and combat operations.  Assignments may also include commanding an
ammunition company.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) 2340 LDOs must achieve selection by the board.
(2) Security requirement:  secret security clearance eligibility.
(3) This MOS may only be assigned to Warrant Officers who were
previously qualified in MOS 2311.
(4) Must meet eligibility and appointment criteria as prescribed in
MCO 1040.42B, WO and LDO accession programs.
(5) Only MOS 2340 warrant officers may apply for appointment as a MOS
2340 limited duty officer (LDO).  Warrant Officers applying for MOS 2340 LDO
must have demonstrated ammunition technical qualification through performance
of duty as a warrant officer in ammunition billets.
c.  Requirements
(1) Warrant Officers must complete the Warrant Officer Basic Course
(WOBC) (M02RMN4) within 18 months of appointment.
(2) Complete the Advanced Ammunition Operations Planner Course
(A14GPY6), U.S. Army Ordnance Missile and Munitions Center and School, Ft
Gregg-Adams, VA.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (bz), Ammunition Technician/Officer Training and Readiness Manual.

## 1117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-106 Enclosure (1)
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## None.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

## 1118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-107 Enclosure (1)
## 1118.  OCCUPATIONAL FIELD 26, SIGNALS INTELLIGENCE/ELECTRONIC WARFARE/
## CYCBERSPACE OPERATIONS
-  MOS 2602, Signals Intelligence/Electronic Warfare/Cyberspace Operations
Officer (III) (CWO5 to WO) PMOS, DIR INTEL
a.  Summary.  Signals Intelligence (SIGINT), Electronic Warfare (EW),
Cyberspace Operations (CO) Warrant Officers supervise, direct, and provide
guidance and technical expertise on all aspects of the employment of SIGINT/
EW/CO personnel, systems and facilities.  These operations are in support of
tactical, strategic, and national level SIGINT/EW/CO missions and include the
management, collection, processing, analysis, and reporting of SIGINT and
electronic warfare support (ES) derived intelligence and information,
directing and supervising offensive cyberspace (OCO), planning and directing
electronic attack (EA) and ES missions; and other tasks related to security
and dissemination of SIGINT/EW/CO derived information.  Marines in this MOS
may be assigned to a BIC (within their occupational field) that has been
coded with one or more DoD Cyber Workforce Framework (DCWF) work role codes.
Please reference DoDI 8140.10, DoDM 8140.10, and the MOS roadmap for
additional training and certification requirements associated with these work
role codes.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must successfully complete a counter-intelligence scope polygraph
examination.
(2) Must have previously served in one of the following MOSs 2611,
2621, 2629, 2631, 2641, or 2651.
(3) Must meet eligibility and appointment criteria as prescribed in
MCO 1040.42B, WO and LDO accession programs.
(4) Must have completed the Defense Acquisition University class CLR
101, Introduction to Joint Capabilities Integration and Development System
## (JCIDS).
(5) Security requirement:  must have a current single scope
background investigation/T5 investigation and adjudicated for sensitive
compartmented information (SCI) eligibility.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Warrant Officer Basic Course (WOBC) (M02RMN4) within
18 months of appointment to WO.
(2) Complete the SIGINT/EW/Cyberspace Operations Officer Course
(N46B122), Marine Detachment Dam Neck, Virginia Beach, VA.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer reference
## (p), Signals Intelligence Training & Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Military Officer Special and Tactical Operations Leaders/Managers, All Other
## 55-1019.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-108 Enclosure (1)
## 1119.  OCCUPATIONAL FIELD 27, LINGUIST
-  MOS 2712, Arabic (Modern Standard) (II/III) (Gen to 2ndLt) and (CWO5 to
## WO) EMOS, DIR INTEL
a.  Summary.  LinLinguist MOSs are EMOSs used in conjunction with
designated billets that indicate specialized foreign language skills.
Linguists supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation
activities pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence
activities.  The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those
Marines that have attended formal language training and have achieved
qualifying foreign language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to
identify billets in T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as
required by the BMOS or additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Arabic (Modern Standard).
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
Primary MOS, Billet MOS or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other Citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the
person interviewed, for use by unit commander and for dissemination to lower,
adjacent, and higher headquarters.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-109 Enclosure (1)
(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written
material of both a technical and nontechnical nature.
(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and
foreign language dictionaries, and glossaries of military terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.
f.  Related Military Skill

a.  Summary.  Linguist MOSs are EMOSs used in conjunction with designated
billets that indicate specialized foreign language skills.  Linguists
supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Swahili.
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-119 Enclosure (1)
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other Citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the
person interviewed, for use by unit commander and for dissemination to lower,
adjacent, and higher headquarters.
(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written
material of both a technical and nontechnical nature.
(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and
foreign language dictionaries, and glossaries of military terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target

(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar

(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as

(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the

(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written

f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

-  MOS 2730, Arabic (Yemeni) (II/III) (Gen to 2ndLt) and (CWO5 to WO) EMOS,
## DIR INTEL
a.  Summary.  Linguist MOSs are EMOSs used in conjunction with designated
billets that indicate specialized foreign language skills.  Linguists
supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-123 Enclosure (1)
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Arabic (Yemeni).
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts interpretation and translation of foreign language to
English and English to foreign language using phraseology selected to
preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, or
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker as to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in meetings, working parties, or similar non-technical
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) May be required to assist in the writing of reports, including
opinion as to the credibility of the person interviewed, for use by unit
commander and for dissemination to lower, adjacent, and higher headquarters.
(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written
material of a non-technical nature.
(8) Establishes an archive file of language reference materials and
lexical aids such as English to foreign language and foreign language to
English dictionaries, cultural dictionaries, and glossaries of military
terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-124 Enclosure (1)
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-131 Enclosure (1)
-  MOS 2738, Indonesian (II/III) (Gen to 2ndLt) and (CWO5 to WO) EMOS, DIR
## INTEL
a.  Summary.  Linguist MOSs are EMOSs used in conjunction with designated
billets that indicate specialized foreign language skills.  Linguists
supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Indonesian.
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other Citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the
person interviewed, for use by unit commander and for dissemination to lower,
adjacent, and higher headquarters.
(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written
material of both a technical and nontechnical nature.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-132 Enclosure (1)
(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and
foreign language dictionaries, and glossaries of military terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

-  MOS 2749, Yakan (II/III) (Gen to 2ndLt) and (CWO5 to WO) EMOS, DIR INTEL
a.  Summary.  Linguist MOSs are EMOSs used in conjunction with designated
billets that indicate specialized foreign language skills.  Linguists
supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient Yakan.
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-144 Enclosure (1)
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or target language the statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the
person interviewed, for use by unit commander and for dissemination to lower,
adjacent, and higher headquarters.
(7) Translates into either English or the target language, written
material of both a technical and nontechnical nature.
(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and
foreign language dictionaries, and glossaries of military terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 2754, Dutch (II/III) (Gen to 2ndLt) and (CWO5 to WO) EMOS, DIR INTEL
a.  Summary.  Linguist MOSs are EMOSs used in conjunction with designated
billets that indicate specialized foreign language skills.  Linguists
supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Dutch.


## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-145 Enclosure (1)
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the
person interviewed, for use by unit commander and for dissemination to lower,
adjacent, and higher headquarters.
(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written
material of both a technical and nontechnical nature.
(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and
foreign language dictionaries, and glossaries of military terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

## INTEL
a.  Summary.  Linguist MOSs are EMOSs used in conjunction with designated
billets that indicate specialized foreign language skills.  Linguists
supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Spanish.
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other citizens to obtain information of military
value.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-157 Enclosure (1)
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the
person interviewed, for use by unit commander and for dissemination to lower,
adjacent, and higher headquarters.
(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written
material of both a technical and nontechnical nature.
(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and
foreign language dictionaries, and glossaries of military terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be efficient in Estonian.


## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-169 Enclosure (1)
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the
person interviewed, for use by unit commander and for dissemination to lower,
adjacent, and higher headquarters.
(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written
material of both a technical and nontechnical nature.
(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and
foreign language dictionaries, and glossaries of military terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.

(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate

reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties

language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar

(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the

(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and

e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.

The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Slovenian.
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.

## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-182 Enclosure (1)
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as
police, clergymen, and other citizens to obtain information of military
value.
(6) Writes reports, including opinion as to the credibility of the
person interviewed, for use by unit commander and for dissemination to lower,
adjacent, and higher headquarters.
(7) Translates into either English or the foreign language, written
material of both a technical and nontechnical nature.
(8) Establishes a file of language reference materials, English and
foreign language dictionaries, and glossaries of military terms.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 2794, Ukrainian (II/III) (Gen to 2ndLt) and (CWO5 to WO) EMOS, DIR
## INTEL
a.  Summary.  Linguist MOSs are EMOSs used in conjunction with designated
billets that indicate specialized foreign language skills.  Linguists
supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Ukrainian.
c.  Requirements
(1) Qualifying DLPT scores alone are not justification for EMOS
assignment.  Marines must successfully complete the required course of
instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS
when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).
(2) Maintain minimum ILR proficiency as required by the appropriate
primary MOS, billet MOS, or designated billet in two modalities of listening,
reading, or speaking portions of the DLPT.

## 1-183 Enclosure (1)
d.  Duties
(1) Conducts bi-directional consecutive interpretation of target
language to English and English to target language using phraseology selected
to preserve the original intent, meaning, and emphasis.
(2) Phrases ideas to avoid language that is stilted, unnatural, and
difficult to understand.
(3) Displays an impartial attitude toward each speaker and to facts
and opinions expressed.
(4) Interprets into either English or foreign language statements
made by participants in conferences, working parties, and all similar
activities.
(5) Interviews friendly non-English speaking civilians such as

Interpreters and Translators 27-3091.

supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that

language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).

instruction at the Defense Language Institute, Language Training Detachment,
or designated government-funded language course and achieve minimum ILR
proficiency as required by the appropriate PMOS or NMOS in two modalities of
listening, reading or speaking portions of the Defense Language Proficiency
Test (DLPT).  (Note:  By exception, individuals may qualify for this EMOS

when language training is not required but Marine meets proficiency
requirements and language fulfills PMOS, NMOS or billet requirements).

supervise and participate in language translation/interpretation activities
pertaining primarily to military operational and intelligence activities.
The linguist MOSs will be assigned as EMOSs to identify those Marines that
have attended formal language training and have achieved qualifying foreign
language skills.  The titles and MOSs will be used to identify billets in
T/Os requiring specific foreign language skills as required by the BMOS or
additional skills designator (ASD).
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be proficient in Bengali.


## 1119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 1-185 Enclosure (1)
