(3) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Construction Trades and
## Extraction Workers 47-1011.

## 3117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-112 Enclosure (1)
(4) Welding, Soldering and Brazing Workers 51-4120.
f.  Related Military Skill.  Aircraft Welder, 6043.
-  MOS 1341, Engineer Equipment Mechanic (SSgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Engineer Equipment Mechanics perform preventive maintenance
and perform repairs to diesel engines and gasoline and diesel driven
construction equipment such as material handling equipment, earth moving
equipment, construction equipment and other engine driven or towed equipment.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must have normal color vision.
(2) Meet the physical requirements of reference (ax).
(3) Must possess an EL score of 90 or MM score of 90 or higher.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Engineer Equipment Mechanic (USMC) Course (A16AD21)
Marine Corps Detachment, U.S. Army Engineer School, Ft Leonard Wood, MO.
(2) Complete the Construction Equipment Repairer Course (A1613B1)
Marine Corps Detachment, U.S. Army Engineer School, Ft Leonard Wood, MO.
(3) Noncommissioned Officers must complete the Engineer Equipment
Mechanic NCO Course (A16ACU1) prior to promotion to SSgt.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (l), Marine Corps Engineer and Utilities Training and Readiness
## Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) Bus and Truck Mechanics and Diesel Engine Specialists 49-3031.
(2) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Construction Trades and
## Extraction Workers 47-1011.
(3) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Mechanics, Installers, and
## Repairers 49-1011.
## (4) Mobile Heavy Equipment Mechanics, Except Engines 49-3042.
## (5) Maintenance Workers, Machinery 49-9043.
(6) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Transportation and Material-
Moving Machine and Vehicle Operators 53-1031.
f.  Related Military Skill
(1) Main Battle Tank Repairer/Technician, 2146
## (2) Automotive Maintenance Technician, 3521.


## 3117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-113 Enclosure (1)
-  MOS 1342, Small Craft Mechanic (SSgt to LCpl) NMOS (1341), DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Small Craft Mechanics perform preventive maintenance and
repairs to Marine Corps-owned watercraft, boats, and rubber-hulled vessels;
repair diesel engines, outboard engines, and hydro jet propulsion systems;
and repair craft trailers and cradle systems owned by the Marine Corps.
Examples are long-range unmanned surface vessels, Marine Corps open water
safety craft, or craft operated or owned by Recon, MARSOC, and all FMF units.
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be qualified in MOS 1341.
c.  Requirements.  Complete the Small Craft Mechanic Course (M03EAW2)
Marine Corps Engineer School, Camp Lejeune, NC.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (l), Marine Corps Engineer and Utilities Training and Readiness
## Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
## (1) Motorboat Mechanic 49-3051.
(2) Outdoor Power Equipment and other Small Engine Mechanics 49-3053.
## (3) Maintenance Workers, Machinery 49-9043.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Engineer Equipment Mechanic, 1341.
## (2) Engineer Equipment Chief, 1349.
-  MOS 1345, Engineer Equipment Operator (SSgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Engineer Equipment Operators operate gasoline or diesel
engine equipment used in material handling and earthmoving operations, to
include all equipment accessories.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Normal color perception.
(2) Height requirement of 66 inches.
(3) Must possess an MM score of 95 or higher.
(4) Must meet the licensing requirements of reference (ax).
(5) Vision correctable to 20/20, and normal depth perception.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Engineer Equipment Operator Course (A1613F1) at
Marine Corps Detachment, U.S. Army Engineer School, Ft Leonard Wood, MO.
(2) Noncommissioned Officers must complete the Advanced Engineer
Equipment Operations Course (A16ACX1) prior to promotion to SSgt.

## 3117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-114 Enclosure (1)
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (l), Marine Corps Engineer and Utilities Training and Readiness
## Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Construction Trades and
## Extraction Workers 47-1011.
(2) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Mechanics, Installers, and
## Repairers 49-1011.
(3) Bus and Truck Mechanics and Diesel Engine Specialists 49-3031.
## (4) Mobile Heavy Equipment Mechanics, Except Engines 49-3042.
## (5) Maintenance Workers, Machinery 49-9043.
(6) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Transportation and Material-
Moving Machine and Vehicle Operators 53-1031.
f.  Related Military Skill.  Engineer Equipment Mechanic, 1341.
-  MOS 1349, Engineer Equipment Chief (MGySgt to GySgt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Engineer Equipment Chiefs coordinate and manage the
maintenance and employment of engineer equipment in both horizontal
construction and material handling operations.  They manage equipment
licensing, certification of tactical ground load lifting equipment, equipment
repairs, and related metal working in support of mobility, counter-mobility,
general, combat engineering, and logistics operations in any environment.
Additionally, they manage and coordinate ground maintenance, maintenance
management policy, programs, procedures, functions, maintenance automated
information systems (MAIS) requirements, and the support of engineer
equipment and watercraft.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess an MM score of 95 or higher.
(2) Possess experience in either MOS 1316, 1341, or 1345.
c.  Requirements.  The Engineer Equipment Chief's Course (A1613E1) is not
an MOS producing course and therefore completion of the course is not
required to be assigned MOS 1349 as a primary MOS.  Upon promotion to Gunnery
Sergeant MOS 1349 as a primary MOS will be granted and reported in MCTFS via
a unit diary entry.  However, Marines holding MOS 1349 are required to attend
the Engineer Equipment Chief's Course (A1613E1) prior to promotion to Master
## Sergeant.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (l), Marine Corps Engineer and Utilities Training and Readiness
## Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Construction Trades and
## Extraction Workers 47-1011.

## 3117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-115 Enclosure (1)
(2) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Mechanics, Installers, and
## Repairers 49-1011.
(3) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of Transportation and Material-
Moving Machine and Vehicle Operators 53-1031.
(4) First-Line Supervisors/Managers of All Other Tactical Operations
## Specialist 55-2013.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Ordnance Vehicle Maintenance Chief, 2149.
## (2) Motor Transport Maintenance Chief, 3529.
-  MOS 1361, Engineer Assistant (GySgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Engineer Assistants perform various duties incidental to
construction design, planning, estimating, and management.  Personnel
assigned this MOS are trained to use optical reading/electronic total station
survey instruments to establish the horizontal and vertical alignment/layout
for construction projects.  In addition, they are trained to use manual/
Computer Aided Drafting (CAD) methods of preparing architectural/mechanical/
civil drawings, to include computations for bills of material/earthwork
volumes.
b.  Prerequisites.  Must possess an EL score of 95 or GT score of 95 or
higher.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Technical Engineering Course (A161421, A161435,
A1614D1), U.S. Army Engineer School Ft Leonard Wood, MO.
(2) Upon promotion to Master Sergeant, Engineer Assistants are
assigned MOS 1371.  As such, completion of the Engineer Operations Chief
Course (M0313G2) at Camp Lejeune, NC is required within one year after
selection to the grade of Master Sergeant; unless assigned outside the
supporting establishment in a B billet, such as recruiting/MSG duty.  In
these cases, after reassignment to a billet requiring MOS 1371, Marines will
attend this course within six months.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (l) Marine Corps Engineer and Utilities Training and Readiness
## Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) Architectural and Civil Drafters 17-3011.
(2) Surveying and Mapping Technicians 17-3031.
## (3) Surveyors 17-1022.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Geographic Intelligence Specialist, 0261.

## 3117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-116 Enclosure (1)
## (2) Combat Engineer, 1371.
-  MOS 1371, Combat Engineer (MGySgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Combat Engineers conduct engineer reconnaissance; emplace
obstacle systems, conduct breaching operations, reduce explosive obstacles/
hazards conduct mine/countermine operations, employ demolitions and military
explosives, conduct urban breaching, conduct route and area clearance, non-
standard bridging/repair, construct and maintain combat roads and trails,
construct expedient roads and airfield/landing zones, design and construct
survivability positions, perform expedient vertical and horizontal
construction, design, construct and maintain base camps/forward operating
bases and combat outposts, and fight as provisional infantry.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess an MM score of 95 or higher.
(2) Pass the normal color perception test w/PIP (12 out of 14
correct) or FALANT.
(3) Must meet MOS physical standards, as listed in NAVMC 3500.12
prior to graduating from MOS school.
(4) Must meet MOS classification standards as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(5) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. ix) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
(6) Must undergo computerized neurocognitive assessment testing at
Marine Corps Engineer School (MCES) using the Automated Neuropsychological
Assessment Metrics (ANAM) Test prior to beginning training.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Basic Combat Engineer Course (M031302), Marine Corps
Engineer School, Camp Lejeune, NC.
(2) Complete the Engineer Operations Chief Course (M0313G2), Marine
Corps Engineer School, Camp Lejeune, NC is required upon promotion to MSgt.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (l), Marine Corps Engineer and Utilities Training and Readiness
## Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) Explosives Workers, Ordnance Handling Experts, and Blasters 47-
## 5031.
(2) Cement Masons and Concrete Finishers 47-2051.
(3) Brick Masons and Block Masons 47-2021.
## (4) Carpenters 47-2031.

## 3117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-117 Enclosure (1)
f.  Related Military Skill.  Engineer Assistant, 1361.
-  MOS 1391, Expeditionary Fuels Technician (MGySgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Expeditionary Fuels Technicians install, operate and employ
a family of tactical fuel systems (TFS) and accessory equipment, tailoring
fuel equipment in modular sets to address mission needs in support of both
ground and aviation operations.  This includes the setup and operation of
fuel systems in support to installations, forward operating bases (FOBs) a
forward arming and refueling points (FARPS).  While utilizing tactical fuel
systems, expeditionary fuel technicians can receive, store and dispense
various petroleum products in support of tactical operations.  Additionally,
expeditionary fuels technicians are responsible for overseeing the quality
surveillance procedures of fuel dispensed from TFS, provide support for the
calculation of fuel estimates in support of operations and assist in the
coordination of sources of class III to support expeditionary operations.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess a GT score of 95 or MM score of 95 or higher.
(2) Security requirement:  must have a favorable national agency
check, local agency check, and credit check (NACLC).
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Bulk Fuel Specialist Course (A1430J1, A141351),
Marine Corps Detachment, U.S. Army Quartermaster School, Ft Gregg-Adams, VA.
(2) Completion of the Bulk Fuel Advance Course (A14ECC1), conducted
at Marine Detachment, U.S. Army Quartermaster School, Ft Gregg-Adams, VA is
required before promotion to master sergeant.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (l), Marine Corps Engineer and Utilities Training and Readiness
## Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
## (1) Pump Operators, Except Wellhead Pumpers 53-7072.
(2) Petroleum Pump System Operators, Refinery Operators, and Gaugers
## 51-8093.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 1392, Petroleum Quality Assurance and Additization Specialist (SSgt
to LCpl) NMOS (1391), DC I&L
a.  Summary.  A Petroleum Quality Assurance Specialist is responsible to
oversee, maintain and manage a bulk fuel unit's quality assurance/
surveillance responsibilities.  Additionally, they are primarily responsible
for operating the Petroleum Quality Analysis System Enhanced (PQAS-E) and
establishing internal procedures to properly use field testing kits for both
ground and aviation units.  Furthermore, 1392s are responsible for the
employment of the Expeditionary Mobile Fuel Additization Capability (EMFAC).
This MOS will be assigned as a NMOS only.

## 3117 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-118 Enclosure (1)
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must hold 1391 as a primary MOS.
(2) Sampling requirements computer based training provided by DLA-E.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete both phases of the Petroleum Laboratory Course (USMC):
(a) (A1413H1) Petroleum Laboratory Specialist, U.S. Army
Quartermaster School, Ft Gregg-Adams, VA.
(b) (A1414Y1) Petroleum Laboratory Specialist (USMC), U.S. Army
Quartermaster School, Ft Gregg-Adams, VA.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, consult
reference (i), Engineer Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) Inspectors, testers, sorters, samplers, and weighers 51-9061.
(2) Petroleum Pump System Operators, Refinery Operators, and Gaugers
## 51-8093.00.
f.  Related Military Skill.  Bulk Fuel Specialist, 1391.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-119 Enclosure (1)
## 3118.  OCCUPATIONAL FIELD 17, INFORMATION MANEUVER
-  Introduction.  The Information Maneuver Occupational Field integrates
information capability areas and the military occupational specialties (MOS)
of cyberspace, space, electromagnetic warfare, civil affairs, and
psychological operations into a single OCCFLD.  Marines from these
disciplines utilize their exquisite talents, knowledge, skills, and abilities
to generate, preserve, and apply informational power against a relevant actor
in order to increase or protect competitive advantage or combat power
potential within all domains of the operating environment.  Duty assignments
include Marine Corps Forces Cyberspace Command, Marine Corps Forces Space
## Command, Marine Corps Information Command, Marine Cryptologic Support
Battalion, Marine Corps Information Operations Center, MEF information
groups, HQMC, MARFORs, joint organizations, and staff sections at all levels
of the MAGTF.
-  MOS 1712, Interactive On-Net Operator (MGySgt to Pvt) NMOS (1721, 1799,
## 2629, 2651, 2659), DC I
a.  Summary.  This NMOS requires advanced cyberspace operations
training.  Interactive On-Net Operators are involved in all facets of
planning and delivering cyberspace effects to achieve tactical, operational,
and strategic objectives.  The Interactive On-Net Operator must be an expert
with computer hardware and software programs to include network operating
systems.  The duties for MOS 1712 encompass cyberspace operations,
intelligence operations, and information operations planning support to
develop situational awareness within the cyberspace environment.  Interactive
On-Net Operators are technical experts in offensive cyberspace operations.
The duties for MOS 1712 increase with experience in multiple disciplines to
encompass developing new techniques and methods, technical leadership, and
serving as the subject matter expert for tools, techniques, and procedures.
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be currently assigned to one of the following
PMOSs 1721, 1799, 2629, 2651, or 2659.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete training and certification requirements as determined by
apprentice level national security agency or senior level United States Cyber
Command standards.
(2) MOS 1712 assigned upon approval of the Director, Information
## Maneuver Division.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## None.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Offensive Cyberspace Warfare Officer, 1710.
## (2) Exploitation Analyst, 1713.
## (3) Cyberspace Warfare Operator, 1721.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-120 Enclosure (1)
## (4) Cyberspace Warfare Chief, 1799.
-  MOS 1713, Exploitation Analyst (MGySgt to Pvt) NMOS (1721, 1799, 2629),
## DC I
a.  Summary.  This NMOS requires advanced analytical training.
Exploitation Analysts are the primary technical planners for offensive
cyberspace operations.  The Exploitation Analyst must be an expert with
adversary systems, networks, technologies, tools, techniques, and procedures.
The duties for MOS 1713 encompass generating intelligence, converting
intelligence to operational planning, developing significant target
awareness, and balancing levels of risk against operational goals.  Duties
increase with experience to encompass the management of target packages and
developing new techniques and procedures for offensive cyber operations.
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be currently assigned to one of the following
PMOSs 1721, 1799, or 2629.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete training and certification standards as determined by
the national security agency or United States Cyber Command.
(2) MOS 1713 assigned upon approval of the Director, Information
## Maneuver Division.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## None.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Offensive Cyberspace Warfare Officer, 1710.
(2) Interactive On-Net Operator, 1712.
## (3) Cyberspace Warfare Operator, 1721.
## (4) Cyberspace Warfare Chief, 1799.
-  MOS 1721, Cyberspace Warfare Operator (GySgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I
a.  Summary.  The Cyberspace Warfare Operator is familiar with all
aspects of cyberspace maneuver operations.  They employ offensive and
defensive cyber tools, tactics, techniques, and procedures.  They integrate
effects into warfighting functions in order to achieve tactical, operational,
and strategic objectives.  The Cyber Warfare Operator further provides the
means to emulate tactics, techniques, and procedures used by known
adversaries to identify unmitigated vulnerabilities through simulated
exploitation or attacks on Department of Defense information systems.
Familiar with digital network concepts and analytical techniques that support
tactical cyber operations.  Utilizing these skills to enumerate friendly
environments, these operators search for and respond to targeted adversary
presence within friendly terrain.  Marines in this MOS may be assigned to a
BIC within their occupational field that has been coded with one or more DoD
cyber workforce framework (DCWF) work role codes.  Please reference DoDI

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-121 Enclosure (1)
8140.10, DoDM 8140.10, and the MOS roadmap for additional training and
certification requirements associated with these work role codes.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a U.S. citizen.
(2) Must possess a CL score of 110 or GT score of 110 or higher.
(3) Accession Marines must possess a cyber-test score of 55 or
higher.
(4) Must be willing to submit to a counterintelligence scope
polygraph examination.
(5) Lateral move additional requirements:
(a) Must complete NAVMC 11665.
(b) Cyber test score is not required for lateral move.
(c) Must be worldwide deployable at the time SNM is approved for
lateral move into 1721 MOS.
(6) Security requirement:
(a) Must meet sensitive compartmented information (SCI)
eligibility guidelines based on a single scope background T5 investigation.
(b) Must complete SCI Pre-Screen Interview with HQMC SSO for
assignment qualification per DoN and HQMC SCI policies and procedures.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Joint Cyber Analysis Course (N23CUQ1), Pensacola,
## FL.
(2) Complete the Cyber Common Technical Core (CCTC) (A09BNO2), Ft
Eisenhower, GA.
(3) These requirements can be waived by the Deputy Commandant for
Information upon examination of alternate cyber workforce training.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Computer Operators 43-9011.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Network Administrator, 0631.
## (2) Network Chief, 0639.
## (3) Data Systems Administrator, 0671.
## (4) Data Systems Chief, 0679.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-122 Enclosure (1)
(5) Interactive On-Net Operator, 1712.
## (6) Exploitation Analyst, 1713.
## (7) Host Analyst, 1722.
## (8) Network Analyst, 1723.
## (9) Tactical Data Systems Technician, 5974.
(10) Aviation Logistics Information Management System (ALIMS)
## Specialist, 6694.
-  MOS 1722, Host Analyst (MGySgt to Pvt) NMOS (1721, 1799), DC I
a.  Summary.  This NMOS will be granted upon meeting the United States
Cyber Command training and certification standards for the senior
certification level in this work role.  The 1722 NMOS requires knowledge of
operating systems, services, security, and various system configurations
encountered.  This work role performs individual memory process analysis
using built-in tools and capabilities.  The 1722 NMOS possesses advanced
knowledge of Unix/Linux services and their security and configuration.  The
Host Analyst also requires advanced knowledge of file systems, permissions,
and operation system configurations.  The Host Analyst develops highly
tailored queries and data visualizations in order gain a battlespace
situational awareness of the endpoint portion of the cyberspace warfighting
domain.  The 1722 NMOS is responsible for capturing the memory of individual
processes and analyzing it using built-in tools and capabilities.
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be currently assigned the PMOS of 1721 or 1799.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete training and certification standards as determined by
## United States Cyber Command.
(2) MOS 1722 assigned upon approval of the Director, Information
## Maneuver Division.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## None.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Defensive Cyberspace Weapons Officer, 1720.
## (2) Cyberspace Warfare Operator, 1721.
## (3) Network Analyst, 1723.
## (4) Cyberspace Warfare Chief, 1799.



## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-123 Enclosure (1)
-  MOS 1723, Network Analyst (MGySgt to Pvt) NMOS (1721, 1799), DC I
a.  Summary.  This NMOS will be granted upon meeting the United States
Cyber Command training and certification standards for the senior
certification level in this work role.  The 1723 NMOS is responsible for
providing analytic expertise of network traffic.  They will understand
signatures and discover anomalies through network flows and traffic patterns.
The Network Analyst will identify, assess, and mitigate intrusions against
networks that are vital to the commander’s objectives.  The duties for MOS
1723 encompass analyzing full packet captures, assisting in developing
network topology mapping, developing network-based signatures, developing
advanced network detection rules and alerts, and developing highly tailored
queries and data visualizations in order gain a battlespace situational
awareness of the network portion of the cyberspace warfighting domain.  The
Network Analyst identifies Enterprise network flow in order to identify
anomalous traffic, utilizes scripting languages to parse collected data for
analysis, and develops signatures to redirect specific traffic, as desired.
b.  Prerequisites.  Must be currently assigned the PMOS of 1721 or 1799.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete training and certification standards as determined by
## United States Cyber Command.
(2) MOS 1723 assigned upon approval of the Director, Information
## Maneuver Division.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## None.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Defensive Cyberspace Weapons Officer, 1720.
## (2) Cyberspace Warfare Operator, 1721.
## (3) Host Analyst, 1722.
## (4) Cyberspace Warfare Chief, 1799.
-  MOS 1732, Civil Affairs Specialist (MGySgt to Cpl) PMOS, DC I
a.  Summary.  Civil Affairs (CA) Specialists perform various duties in
both combat and non-combat environments involving the integration, planning,
and conduct of civil affairs operations (CAO) and civil-military operations
(CMO) in support of marine air-ground task force (MAGTF) operations.  CA
Specialists inform, influence, shape and gain access to the cognitive civil
environment through targeted and integrated civil reconnaissance and civil
engagements.  This PMOS is only applicable to the reserve component.  The MOS
was previously listed as 0532.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a corporal or above.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-124 Enclosure (1)
(2) Must possess a GT score of 100 or higher.
(3) Security requirement:  secret security clearance eligibility.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Civil Affairs Specialist Course (M02ZSGD) conducted
at MCCMOS, MCB Quantico, VA.
(2) Marines holding MOS 1751 are eligible to be awarded PMOS 1732 by
requesting the change if they are assigned to BMOS 1732.
d.  Duties
(1) Integrate civil affairs activities and tasks (engagements,
assessments, civil reconnaissance, etc.) with supported unit.
(2) Participate in the CMO working group, information operations
working group, targeting board and other B2C2WGs as appropriate.
(3) Provide civil preparation of the battlespace (civil
considerations) into the intelligence preparation of the battlespace and
Marine Corps planning process.
(4) Develop civil affairs plans (engagements, assessments, civil
reconnaissance, etc.) and advise the commander on the conduct of civil
affairs operations (CA activities, military government operations, CA
supported activities) throughout the conflict continuum for all operations,
contingencies, and exercises.
(5) Coordinate and liaise with interagency, host nation,
international, and non-governmental personnel.
(6) Establish relationships to shape, inform, influence, and exploit
within the battlespace and synchronize efforts with information related
capabilities and other elements of the MAGTF to leverage capabilities and
maximize effects.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Emergency Management Specialists 13-1061.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Advisor, 0570.
## (2) Operational Advisor, 0571.
## (3) Influence Specialist, 1751.
-  MOS 1739, Civil-Military Operations (CMO) Chief (MGySgt to Sgt) NMOS
## (1751, 1795), DC I
a.  Summary.  Civil-Military Operations Chiefs assist in the planning,
coordinating, and assessment civil affairs operations (CAO) and civil-
military operations (CMO).  These Staff Noncommissioned Officers assist in
advising MAGTF commanders and their staff sections on the conduct of CMO as
well as in understanding and shaping civil conditions to support MAGTF
operations.  CMO Chiefs assist with integrating CMO into the Marine Corps

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-125 Enclosure (1)
planning process (MCPP) across the range of military operations.  CMO Chiefs
assist in the development of green cell products, and they coordinate, plan
and assist in the developing an Annex G supporting a MAGTF or joint task
force (JTF) operation plan or operations order.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess PMOS 1751, Influence Specialist.
(2) Security requirement:  secret clearance eligibility.
c.  Requirements.  Complete the Civil-Military Operations Planner Chief
Course (M02ZSED), Marine Corps Civil-Military Operations School (MCCMOS),
Quantico, VA.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Emergency Management Specialist 13-1061.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Civil Affairs Specialist, 1732.
## (2) Influence Specialist, 1751.
-  MOS 1751, Influence Specialist (GySgt to Sgt) PMOS, DC I
a.  Summary.  Influence Specialists are the Marines that make up the
PSYOP and civil affairs forces in the active component.  These are the forces
which conduct military information support operations (MISO), civil affairs
operations (CAO), or civil military operations (CMO).  The Influence
Specialists billet will determine their support to plan and execute functions
pertaining to military information support operations (MISO), civil
authorities information support (CAIS), and civil affairs operations (CAO).
Influence Specialists deploy in small teams known as tactical PSYOP teams or
CA teams, depending on the billet they are filling, to employ capabilities
that operationalize the information warfighting function and harness the
military applications of information in all military operations.  Influence
Specialists capabilities are critical to the success of the fleet marine
force (FMF) mission, as they enable commanders to shape the information and
security environment in their areas of responsibility (AOR).
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a U.S. citizen.
(2) Must possess a GT score of 100 or higher.
(3) Security requirement:  secret security clearance eligibility.
(4) Must have a first-class physical fitness test (PFT) and combat
fitness test (CFT).
(5) Must be worldwide deployable when approved for lateral move into
the 1751 PMOS.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-126 Enclosure (1)
(6) Must obligate to 60 months of service when approved for lateral
move into the 1751 PMOS.
(7) Must be a volunteer corporal or sergeant from any MOS.  Sergeants
must not be in zone for promotion.
(8) Must attend an influence specialist screening and assessment and
receive a recommendation for lateral move from the MOS specialist.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Psychological Operations Qualification Course (POQC)
(A05M301), John F. Kennedy Special Warfare Center and School, Ft Liberty, NC.
(2) Complete the Civil Reconnaissance NCO Course (M02AAPD), Marine
Corps Civil-Military Operations School (MCCMOS), Quantico, VA.
(3) Complete the MAGTF Operations in the MAGTF Operations in The
Information Environment Specialist (M02F2KD), Quantico, VA.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A CH1, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Military Enlisted Tactical Operations and Air/Weapons Specialists and Crew
## Members, All Other 55-3019.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Information Specialist, 0551.
## (2) Civil Affairs Specialist, 1732.
(3) Civil-Military Operations Chief, 1739.
-  MOS 1762, System Operator (GySgt to Pvt) NMOS (2621, 2631, 2629), DC I
a.  Summary.  Systems Operators are signal intelligence Marines who have
acquired introductory level training in C5ISRT systems and methods for
countering these systems.  They serve as system operators on a tactical level
team equipped with information warfare technology.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a U.S. citizen.
(2) Security requirement:  Must have a current T5 investigation and
adjudicated for SCI eligibility.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Army Space Control Fundamentals Course (ASCF)
(F25M114), US Army Space and Missile Defense Center of Excellence (USA
SMDCoE), Colorado Springs, CO.
(2) Following completion of ASCF, complete Marine Corps Systems
Command new equipment training program.  MOS 1762 is assigned upon approval
of the Information Maneuver Occupational Field Manager.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-127 Enclosure (1)
(3) This MOS may be awarded to Marines that previously completed the
listed training.
(4) Any of these requirements may be waived by the Deputy Commandant
for Information based on educational or work experience.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Command and Control Center Specialists 55-3015.
f.  Related Military Skill
(1) Mission Commander (Space), 1760.
## (2) Radio Frequency Operator, 1766.
(3) Communications Intelligence/Electronic Warfare Operator, 2621.
(4) Electronic Intelligence/Electronic Warfare Analyst, 2631.
(5) Signals Intelligence/Electronic Warfare Technician, 2629.
-  MOS 1766, Radio Frequency Operator (GySgt to Pvt) NMOS (0627, 0629,
## 2651), DC I
a.  Summary.  Radio Frequency Operators are wideband transmission systems
Marines who have acquired introductory level training in C5ISRT communication
systems.  They serve as radio frequency operators equipped with information
warfare technology and are also responsible for communications systems to
facilitate command and control.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a U.S. citizen.
(2) Security requirement:  must have a current T5 investigation and
adjudicated for SCI eligibility.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Army Space Control Fundamentals Course (ASCF)
(F25M114), US Army Space and Missile Defense Center of Excellence (USA
SMDCoE), Colorado Springs, CO.
(2) Following completion of ASCF, complete Marine Corps Systems
Command new equipment training program or Training Command Space Capabilities
and Mission Planning Course. MOS 1766 is assigned upon approval of the
## Information Maneuver Occupational Field Manager.
(3) This MOS may be awarded to Marines that previously completed the
listed training.
(4) Any of these requirements may be waived by the Deputy Commandant
for Information based on educational or work experience.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-128 Enclosure (1)
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Command and Control Center Specialists 55-3015.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Satellite Transmissions Systems Operator, 0627.
## (2) Transmissions Chief, 0629.
## (3) Electromagnetic Spectrum Management Chief, 0648.
(4) Mission Commander (Space), 1760.
## (5) Systems Operator, 1762.
(6) ISR Systems Engineer, 2651.
-  MOS 1795, Influence Chief (MGySgt to MSgt) PMOS, DC I
a.  Summary.  The Influence Chief is the Senior Staff Non-Commissioned
Officer that directly assists Influence Officers, Civil-Military Operations
Planners, and mentors and advises Influence Specialists.  They manage the
conduct of influence operations and can facilitate the integration of
influences operations with other information activities and force maneuver in
the information environment.  The Influence Chief assists in the planning,
training, deployment, and implementation of capabilities and resources
required to conduct influence operations.  Influence Chiefs possess knowledge
on the joint operational and marine corps planning processes (JOPP/MCPP) in
order to develop integrated influence plans and operations.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess MOS 1751.
(2) Attain promotion to MSgt.
(3) Security requirement:  must meet sensitive compartmented
information (SCI) eligibility guidelines based on a single scope background
investigation/T5 investigation.
c.  Requirements.  For MOS and career progression, Staff Operations in
the Information Environment Planner Course (SOPC) (M02HE5D), Quantico, VA is
highly encouraged.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A CH1, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Military Enlisted Tactical Operations and Air/Weapons Specialists and Crew
## Members, All Other 55-3019.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Information Specialist, 0551.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-129 Enclosure (1)
## (2) Civil Affairs Specialist, 1732.
(3) Civil-Military Operations Chief, 1739.
## (4) Influence Specialist, 1751.
-  MOS 1799, Cyberspace Warfare Chief (MGySgt to MSgt) PMOS, DC I
a.  Summary.  The Cyberspace Warfare Chief is the senior staff non-
commissioned officer who directly assists Cyberspace Warfare Officers and
Cyberspace Weapons Officers and supports all Cyber Warfare Operators.  They
manage the conduct of cyber warfare and possess an understanding of
cyberspace warfare operations.  The Cyberspace Warfare Chief assists in the
planning, training, deployment, and implementation of capabilities and
resources required to conduct cyber operations.  Cyberspace Warfare Chiefs
possess in-depth knowledge of the Joint operational and Marine Corps planning
processes (JOPP and MCPP) to develop integrated cyber warfare plans and
operations.  Marines in this MOS may be assigned to a BIC within their
occupational field that has been coded with one or more DoD Cyber Workforce
Framework (DCWF) work role codes.  Please reference DoDI 8140.10, DoDM
8140.10, and the MOS roadmap for additional training and certification
requirements associated with these work role codes.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess MOS 1721.
(2) Must be willing to submit to a counterintelligence scope
polygraph examination.
(3) Security requirement:
(a) Must complete SCI pre-screen interview with HQMC SSO for
assignment qualification per DoN and HQMC SCI policies and procedures.
(b) Must have a current single scope background T5 investigation
and adjudicated for sensitive compartmented information (SCI) eligibility.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Cyber Operations Planners Course (COPC) (A09BN72) at
Ft Eisenhower, GA.
(2) For MOS and career progression, the Joint Targeting Staff Course
(N46HFS3) is highly encouraged.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to NAVMC
3500.124A, Information Maneuver Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## None.
f.  Related Military Skill
(1) Interactive On-Net Operator, 1712.
## (2) Exploitation Analyst, 1713.

## 3118 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-130 Enclosure (1)
## (3) Cyberspace Warfare Operator, 1721.
## (4) Host Analyst, 1722.
## (5) Network Analyst, 1723.

## 3119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-131 Enclosure (1)
## 3119.  OCCUPATIONAL FIELD 18, TANK, ASSAULT AMPHIBIOUS VEHICLE AND AMPHIBIOUS
## COMBAT VEHICLE
-  Introduction.  The Tank, Assault Amphibious Vehicle and Amphibious Combat
Vehicle OccFld includes operation, employment, maneuver, and maintenance of
tracked vehicles in the combined arms environment during both amphibious
assaults and subsequent land operations ashore.  Qualifications required
include basic mechanical aptitude and the ability to perform harmoniously
with others in the confined area inside combat vehicles.  The duties involved
are incident to the operation, employment, maneuver, and maintenance of tanks
and assault amphibious vehicles.  Formal schooling is provided to Marines at
both the entry level and at the appropriate time in career development.
Marines entering this OccFld receive MOS 1800, Basic Tank and Assault
Amphibious Marine.  After entry into OccFld 18 and assignment of a basic 1800
MOS, personnel specialize in either the M1A1 Tank or the Assault Amphibious
Vehicle are assigned MOS 1812, 1833, or 1834 respectively.
-  MOS 1812, Armor Marine (SSgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC PP&O #
a.  Summary.  THIS IS AN OBSOLETE MOS, NO LONGER IN USE IN THE TABLE OF
## ORGANIZATION OF THE MARINE CORPS.  THIS MOS IS MAINTAINED IN THE MOS MANUAL
BECAUSE IT APPEARS IN THE RECORDS OF MARINES STILL ON ACTIVE DUTY.  As
members of an M1A1 tank crew or unit, Armor Marines perform duties necessary
for the proper maintenance, operation, and employment of the M1A1 Main Battle
Tank (MBT).  These duties include preparing the tank, personnel, and
supporting equipment to support training and combat operations, preparation
of ammunition, acquiring targets, employment of organic tank weapons
utilizing the fire control system, driving the tank, and conducting operator-
level preventive and corrective maintenance.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must have normal color vision.
(2) Must have vision correctable to 20/20.
(3) Must possess a GT score of 90 or higher.
(4) Must have a WS-B water survival qualification.
(5) Security clearance requirements:  must be eligible for a secret
clearance.
(6) Must meet MOS classification standards as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(7) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. xiv) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
c.  Requirements.  Complete the M1A1 Armor Crewmember Course (A03TBM2) at
Ft Moore, GA.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (m), Tank Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Armored Assault Vehicle Crew Members 55-3013.

## 3119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-132 Enclosure (1)
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
# MOS 1812 is no longer a PMOS for gunnery sergeants through master gunnery
sergeants.  When promoted to GySgt, the 1869 PMOS will be awarded after
completing the MOS requirements.  To facilitate M&RA with assignments,
Marines may be assigned using the intended MOS (IMOS) for which they were
selected until they have met the requirements.
-  MOS 1833, Assault Amphibious Vehicle (AAV) Crewmember (MGySgt to Pvt)
## PMOS, DC PP&O
a.  Summary.  An Assault Amphibian Crewmember performs various duties
related to the operation and maintenance of Amphibious Vehicles (AV), to
include tactical employment, gunnery using the onboard weapon systems,
communications, and maneuver.  AA Crewmembers employ their vehicle in support
of Infantry units during ship to objective maneuver and during subsequent
operations ashore.  Typical duties may include preventive and corrective
maintenance, and pre-combat checks and inspections on the AV and its
associated equipment in order to support both amphibious and land operations.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must have normal color vision.
(2) Must have WS-I water survival qualification.
(3) Must have a WS-B(+) water survival qualification.
(4) Must have vision correctable to 20/20 in both eyes.
(5) Must possess an EL score of 90 or GT score of 90 or higher.
(6) Must meet MOS classification standards as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(7) Must possess the psychological and physiological qualifications
required for licensing as an Ordnance Vehicle Operator (see reference (cz)).
(8) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. xiv) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Assault Amphibian Crewmember Course (M10AHY3), Camp
Pendleton, CA.
(2) Prior to being assigned the billet of “Vehicle Commander”,
corporals must complete the Assault Amphibian Vehicle Commander Course
## (M1018Q3).
(3) Prior to being assigned the billet “Company Master Gunner”,
sergeants must complete the U.S. Army Master Gunner Common Core Course
## (A03WZ32).
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (n), Assault Amphibious Vehicle Training and Readiness Manual.

## 3119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-133 Enclosure (1)
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) First-Line Supervisor of Weapons Specialist/Crew Members 55-2012.
## (2) Infantry 55-3016.
## (3) Armored Assault Vehicle Crew Members 55-3013.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Machine Gunner, 0331.
(2) Ground Command and Control (C2) Operations NCO, 8711.
-  MOS 1834, Amphibious Combat Vehicle (ACV) Crewmember (MGySgt to Pvt)
## PMOS, DC PP&O #
a.  Summary.  Amphibious Combat Vehicle Crewmember performs various
duties incident to the operation and maintenance of the vehicle, to include
tactical employment, precision gunnery using on board vehicle weapon systems,
and maneuver.  ACV Crewmembers employ their vehicle during shore to shore,
ship to objective maneuver, and during subsequent operations ashore.  Typical
duties may include preparing the ACV and associated equipment for movement
and combat, locating, and engaging targets, driving the ACV in the water and
ashore, and performance of preventive and corrective maintenance.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must have normal color vision.
(2) Must have a WS-B(+) water survival qualification.
(3) Must have vision correctable to 20/20 in both eyes.
(4) Security requirement:  must be secret security eligible.
(5) Must possess an EL score of 90 or GT score of 90 or higher.
(6) Must meet MOS classification standards as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(7) Must possess the psychological and physiological qualifications
required for licensing as an Ordnance Vehicle Operator; see reference (cz).
(8) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. xiv) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
(9) Must have a valid state driver’s license and a past driving
record appropriate for issue of SF-86 as evidenced by a search of the
national driver register.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Amphibious Combat Vehicle Basic Crewmember Course
(M10AH23), Camp Pendleton, CA.

## 3119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-134 Enclosure (1)
(2) Complete the Amphibious Combat Vehicle Operational New Equipment
Training 2.0, licensed by the program manager.
(3) Entry level and lateral move Marines must complete the Amphibious
Combat Vehicle Basic Crewmember Course (ACV-BC) for assignment as a primary
## MOS.
(4) Must attain WS-I water survival qualification prior to completion
of the Amphibious Combat Vehicle Basic Crewmember Course (ACV-BC).
(5) Must complete the Submerged Vehicle Egress Trainer (SVET)
Qualification prior to completion of the Amphibious Combat Vehicle Basic
Crewmember Course (ACV-BC).
(6) Corporals must complete the Assault Amphibian Vehicle Commander
## Course.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (n), Assault Amphibious Vehicle Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code
(1) First-Line Supervisor of Weapons Specialist/Crew Members 55-2012.
## (2) Infantry 55-3016.
## (3) Armored Assault Vehicle Crew Members 55-3013.
(4) First-Line Supervisors of all Other Tactical Operations
## Specialist 55-2013.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Infantry Marine, 0311.
## (2) Light Armored Reconnaissance Marine, 0313.
(3) Ground Command and Control (C2) Operations NCO, 8711.
# MOS 1834: For Marines that are licensed on an amphibious combat vehicle in
the legacy operational new equipment training, or the AAV/ACV hybrid course
provided by assault amphibian school are required to complete the evaluation
program signed off on by the officer in charge of the training transition
unit in order to be awarded the 1834 PMOS.
-  MOS 1867, M1A1 Tank Master Gunner (MGySgt to Sgt) NMOS (1812, 1869), DC
## PP&O
a.  Summary.  THIS IS AN OBSOLETE MOS, NO LONGER IN USE IN THE TABLE OF
## ORGANIZATION OF THE MARINE CORPS.  THIS MOS IS MAINTAINED IN THE MOS MANUAL
BECAUSE IT APPEARS IN THE RECORDS OF MARINES STILL ON ACTIVE DUTY.  The M1A1
Tank Master Gunner is the subject matter expert in the employment and
training of all weapon systems organic to a tank battalion.  Tank Master
Gunners are special staff officers responsible to the commanders at all
levels and staff in the planning, development, conduct, evaluation and
monitoring of all live fire and simulations training program.  They are
responsible for the unit’s ammunition allocation, forecasting, scheduling of
ranges and the certification of evaluators who assist in their duties.  They

## 3119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-135 Enclosure (1)
advise and mentor the officers and marines of the unit in all applicable
mechanical, doctrinal, and conceptual weaponeering and training matters as
required to improve the effectiveness and proficiency of the command.
Additional duties include tank platoon master gunner ISO of a MEU, provide
input in new weapon systems, gear research and development, coordinate new
equipment training (NET), participate in applicable course curriculum review
boards (CCRB), new curriculum development for gunnery and tank related tasks
and vetting of draft tank training and readiness manual (T&R).
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a sergeant or above.
(2) Must have normal color vision.
(3) Must have vision correctable to 20/20.
(4) Must have 6 months experience as a tank commander.
(5) Security requirement:  must be eligible for a secret clearance.
(6) Must have qualified tank table VI as tank commander within the
preceding 12 months.
(7) Must have a minimum GT score of 105 (waivered to 100 based on
qualified throughput availability).
(8) Must have attended Marine Tank Commanders Course (MTCC)
(A030MZM).  This prerequisite is waiverable for SMCR Marines.
(9) Must have completed the M1A1 Marine Armor Crewmember Course
(A03TBM2) at Ft Moore, GA and obtain the PMOS of 1812.
(10) TIS requirements are no less than 4 years and must have at least
24 months left in their contract upon completion of the course.
(11) Must be certified by the Battalion Master Gunner (BMG) that the
Marine has passed the Vehicle Commander's Gunnery Skills Test (GST) as stated
verbatim in the reference within three months or 90 days prior to attending
the M1A1 Abrams Master Gunner Course.
c.  Requirements.  Must have completed the legacy M1A1 Master Gunner
Course (A03TBH2) or complete the M1A2 SEP Master Gunner Course (A03H5T2) at
Ft Moore, GA.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer reference
(m) Tank Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Tactical Ground Range Control Officer.
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Light Armored Reconnaissance Master Gunner, 0367.
## (2) Armor Marine, 1812.
(3) Senior Armor SNCO, 1869.

## 3119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-136 Enclosure (1)
-  MOS 1868, Assault Amphibious Master Gunner (MGySgt to Sgt) NMOS (1833),
## DC PP&O
a.  Summary.  The Marine AA Master Gunner is an MGySgt to Sgt
specifically trained in the employment and training of AA Company and
Battalion organic weapons, gear and assigned personnel and in the basics of
AA Gunnery as defined by the current version of TC 3-20.31 Training and Crew
Qualification.  The battalion master gunners and company master gunners are
special staff officers employed as the principal advisor to their respective
commander.  They assist in the development of training and employment plans
designed to ensure mission essential task compliance.  They help design and
vet the gunnery and training policies of the commander and help to
disseminate information to the unit’s personnel regarding such policies.
They generate and quantify reports on the unit’s technical and tactical
gunnery proficiency and brief the unit commander as to where each subordinate
unit sits in regard to his intent.  They advise/mentor the officers and
Marines of the unit in all applicable mechanical, doctrinal and conceptual
gunnery and training matters as required.  Ultimately, they will provide
improvements to the general efficiency, effectiveness and proficiency of the
command.  They have oversight of the unit's ammunition allocation and semi-
annual gunnery qualification and certification programs.  Additional duties
will include AA platoon master gunner ISO of a MEU, participation in
applicable course curriculum review boards (CCRB), new curriculum development
for gunnery and AA related tasks and vetting within the AA Training and
Readiness Manual (T&R).
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a sergeant or above.
(2) Must have normal color vision.
(3) Must have vision correctable to 20/20.
(4) Must have a minimum GT score of 105 (waivered to 100 based on
qualified throughput availability).
(5) Must have completed the Amphibian Assault Crewman Course
(M10AHY3), Camp Pendleton CA and obtain the PMOS of 1833.
(6) TIS requirements are 5 years but may be adjusted annually by
master gunner conference based on the needs of the AA community.
c.  Requirements.  Must complete the Stryker Master Gunner Course
(A03T2Q1) Stryker Master Gunner, Ft Moore, GA.
d.  Duties.  The AA Master Gunner is the commander’s AA gunnery tables’
technical advisor.  He assists the commander and staff in planning,
developing, conducting and monitoring the unit’s tank gunnery tables program
to include turret maintenance programs.  This will further ensure proper
readiness posture is being maintained.  The master gunner knowledge and
technical expertise will allow him to work directly with individual AA
commanders.  Marines entering this OccFld receive the MOS 1868.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## None.

## 3119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-137 Enclosure (1)
f.  Related Military Skill.  Light Armored Reconnaissance Master Gunner,
## 0367.
-  MOS 1869, Senior Armor SNCO (MGySgt to GySgt) PMOS, DC PP&O # #
a.  Summary.  THIS IS AN OBSOLETE MOS, NO LONGER IN USE IN THE TABLE OF
## ORGANIZATION OF THE MARINE CORPS.  THIS MOS IS MAINTAINED IN THE MOS MANUAL
BECAUSE IT APPEARS IN THE RECORDS OF MARINES STILL ON ACTIVE DUTY.  The
Senior Armor SNCO serves as the senior enlisted technical and tactical
advisor to the commander on tank operations.  Additionally, he advises the
commander on the condition, care and economical use of unit resources and
equipment.  The Senior Armor SNCO coordinates and supervises all aspects of
the platoon, company or battalion operations, logistics and the
administrative requirements that affect unit readiness.  He assists with the
development of the unit’s training plan and management of the unit readiness
program to ensure all task support the unit’s core and assigned mission
essential tasks.  The Senior Armor SNCO serves as the principal mentor to all
marines in the armor community.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must hold PMOS 1812, Armor Marine.
(2) Security requirement:  secret security clearance eligibility.
(3) Marines seeking an MOS lateral move (LAT move) to MOS 1869 are
required to meet all prerequisites and requirements of PMOS 1812.
c.  Requirements
(1) Gunnery Sergeants:  successful completion of the Maneuver Senior
Leader’s Course (MSL-C) (A03KKX3) prior to being assigned the 1869 PMOS.
(2) Master Sergeants:  successful completion of the GCE Operations
Specialist Course (N30RCC1 or N30KBC1) or successful completion of the
Operations and Tactics Instructor Chief Course (M09HE35) prior to being
assigned the 1869 PMOS.
(3) Master Gunnery Sergeants:  successful completion of the
Operations and Tactics Instructor Course (M09HE35 or M09KYD5) prior to being
assigned the 1869 PMOS.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (m), Tank Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
## Armored Assault Vehicle Crew Members 55-3013.
f.  Related Military Skill
(1) Operations and Tactics Instructor, 0577.
## (2) Armor Marine, 1812.
(3) GCE Operations Specialist, 8711.
# The 1869 PMOS will be awarded only after completing the MOS requirements.
To facilitate M&RA with assignments, Marines may be assigned using the

## 3119 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-138 Enclosure (1)
intended MOS (IMOS) for which they were selected until they have met the
requirements.

## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-139 Enclosure (1)
## 3120.  OCCUPATIONAL FIELD 21, GROUND ORDNANCE MAINTENANCE
-  Introduction.  The Ground Ordnance Maintenance OccFld maintains the
serviceability of ground ordnance materials.  Duties include the inspection,
repair, and maintenance of ground ordnance weapons and optical systems
possessed by Marine Corps units.  Qualifications required include basic
ordnance administration and knowledge; the capability to technically inspect/
analyze ordnance items and repair/fabricate the same, and to understand and
implement operational repair facility procedures.  Ground Ordnance
maintainers impart technical knowledge onto organizational personnel, crews,
and supervisors for proper corrective and preventive maintenance, inspection,
and application of weapons and optical systems and all associated support
equipment.  Ground Ordnance Maintenance personnel are required to learn
ground ordnance maintenance administrative procedures, repair analysis,
technical inspection procedures, repair procedures, testing of ordnance
equipment, and quality control methods.  Entry level Occupations include
Small Arms Repairer/Technician, Precision Weapons Repairer/Technician, Towed
Artillery Systems Technician, Assault Amphibious Vehicle (AAV)/Assault Combat
Vehicle (ACV) Repairer/Technician, Heavy Ordnance Vehicle Repairer/
Technician, Light Armored Vehicle (LAV) Repairer/Technician, Machinist, and
Electro-Optical Ordnance Repairer.  Senior enlisted Marines within the MOS
are tasked with overseeing the inspection, repair, and maintenance of ground
ordnance weapons and optical systems.  Marines entering this OccFld will
receive MOS 2100, Basic Ground Ordnance Maintenance Marine.  Marines within
this occupation are familiar with associated warranties and contract
logistics support options for ground ordnance equipment and able to execute
processes and procedures as part of weapons and optics systems sustainment
plans.
-  MOS 2111, Small Arms Repairer/Technician (GySgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  Small Arms Repairer/Technician in the grade of Private
through Lance Corporal, under supervision, inspect, maintain, and repair
small arm weapon systems, less permanently mounted vehicular weapons.  The
Small Arms Repairer/Technician also completes basic shop administrative
actions utilizing forms, records, technical manuals, directives, and
automated information systems.  At the ranks of Corporal through Gunnery
Sergeant, the Small Arms Repairer/Technician performs maintenance actions,
trains subordinates, and supervises the inspection, maintenance, and repair
of all small arms, less permanently mounted vehicular weapons.  The Small
Arms Repairer/Technician also performs, maintains, and supervises shop
maintenance management programs and coordinates the maintenance and repair of
small arms according to grade.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must be a U.S. citizen.
(2) Must possess an MM score of 100 or higher.
(3) Must pass the command arms ammunition, and explosive (AA&E)
screening.
(4) No conviction by court-martial, civilian court or nonjudicial
punishment of any act involving larceny, theft, or drugs.

## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-140 Enclosure (1)
(5) Security requirement:  Must have a national agency check, local
agency check, and credit check (NACLC) resident within the Joint Personnel
Adjudication System (JPAS).
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Small Arms/Towed Artillery Repairer Course (A14AN16)
and the Small Arms Repairer Course (A1421M6), U.S. Army Ordnance School, Ft
Gregg-Adams, VA.
(2) Corporals to sergeants will complete the Ground Ordnance
Maintenance Supervisors Course (A14GBC6) and must remain on active duty for
12 months upon completion of the course.
(3) Staff Sergeants to gunnery sergeants will complete the Ground
Ordnance Managers Course (A14GBS6) and must remain on active duty for 12
months upon completion of the course.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Installation, Maintenance, and Repair Workers, All Other 49-9099.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 2112, Precision Weapons Repairer/Technician (GySgt to Cpl) NMOS
## (2111), DC I&L
a.  Summary.  The Precision Weapons Repairer/Technician performs duties
incident to inspection, maintenance, fabrication, build and repair of
precision small arms in support of the Competition-in-Arms Program , depot
maintenance intra-service support agreement program, and the operating
forces.  This MOS will be assigned as a NMOS only.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must hold PMOS 2111.
(2) Must have a minimum MM score of 105.
(3) Must have at least three years TIS and no more than 12 years TIS.
(4) Must be at least the rank of corporal but no higher than the rank
of staff sergeant.
(5) Must be eligible to execute permanent change of station orders to
attend the Precision Weapons Repair Course (M022199).
(6) Must have at least three years of obligated service remaining
upon completion of the Precision Weapons Repair Course (PWRC).
c.  Requirements
(1) Must submit a completed Precision Weapons Repair Course (M022199)
screening checklist to the course chief, PWRC.

## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-141 Enclosure (1)
(2) Must submit a letter of recommendation from a Precision Weapons
Technician/Ordnance Chief/Ordnance Officer or an SNCO/Officer in the Marine’s
chain of command to the course chief, PWRC.
(3) Must successfully complete the NMOS 2112 Precision Weapons Repair
Course (M022199).
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Installation, Maintenance, and Repair Workers, All Other 49-9099.
f.  Related Military Skill
(1) Small Arms Repairer/Technician, 2111.
## (2) Machinist, 2161.
-  MOS 2131, Artillery Systems Technician (GySgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  The Artillery Systems Technician, under supervision,
performs basic duties incident to inspection, maintenance, and repair of
towed artillery howitzers.  The Artillery Systems Technician also completes
basic shop administrative forms and records utilizing technical manuals.  At
the gunnery sergeant through corporal level, the Artillery Systems Technician
performs, trains, and supervises various duties incident to inspection,
maintenance and repair of towed artillery howitzers.  The Artillery Systems
Technician also prepares, maintains and supervises a shop maintenance and
management program, and performs the maintenance and repair of towed
artillery howitzers according to grade.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess an MM score of 95 or higher.
(2) Must meet MOS classification standards as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(3) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. xiv) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Towed Artillery Repairer Course (A1408O6).
(2) Corporals to sergeants will complete the Ground Ordnance
Maintenance Supervisors Course (A14GBC6) and must remain on active duty for
12 months upon completion of the course.
(3) Staff Sergeants to gunnery sergeants will complete the Ground
Ordnance Managers Course (A14GBS6) and must remain on active duty for 12
months upon completion of the course.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness.

## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-142 Enclosure (1)
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Installation, Maintenance, and Repair Workers, All Other 49-9099.
f.  Related Military Skill.  None.
-  MOS 2141, Assault Amphibious Vehicle (AAV)/Assault Combat Vehicle (ACV)
Repairer/Technician (GySgt to Pvt) PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  The AAV/ACV Repairer/Technician performs basic automotive
duties including the inspection, maintenance, and repair of the AAV/ACV
family of vehicles.  The AAV/ACV Repairer/Technician performs basic shop
administrative actions utilizing technical manuals and automated information
systems.  The AAV/ACV Repairer/Technician also operates the AAV/ACV recovery
vehicle in support of combat and/or garrison operations.  As a member of the
recovery crew, the AAV/ACV Repairer/Technician will perform operator and
field-level maintenance and maintain/manage associated tools and equipment,
of which licensed to operate.  Accordingly, they will employ land navigation
techniques and must be knowledgeable with the operation of radios and weapon
systems when mounted on the vehicle during tactical operations.  The gunnery
sergeant to corporal level supervises, and performs various duties incident
to the inspection, maintenance, and repair of the automotive and turret
systems of the AAV.  In addition, AAV Repairer/Technicians prepare, maintain
and supervise shop maintenance and management programs, and coordinate
maintenance and recovery operations according to grade and position.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess an MM score of 105 or higher.
(2) Must have WS-B(+) water survival qualification.
(3) Must meet MOS classification standards as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(4) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. xiv) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
(5) No more than one yes in the blocks contained in section 6 on the
physical fitness inquiry for motor vehicle operators form (OF-345) is
acceptable.
(6) Must have normal vision to include color, acuity, field of vision
and depth perception.  The examinee will not be disqualified for a vehicle
operator’s license because of color blindness or depth perception.  However,
if color blindness or depth perception is indicated, he or she will be given
additional training, which will enable him or her to drive safely.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Assault Amphibious Vehicle Repairer Basic Course
(M1018Y3), Camp Pendleton, CA or the Amphibious Combat Vehicle Basic Repairer
Course (M10AH33) Camp Pendleton, CA.
(2) Must possess the qualifications required for licensing as an
ordnance vehicle operator per MCO 8400.6_.

## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-143 Enclosure (1)
(3) In order to remain fit for duty, must have a current WSI swim
qualification.
(4) Corporals through gunnery sergeants are expected to have
completed the Assault Amphibian Vehicle Repairer Intermediate Course
(M10AHX3) and have a minimum of 12 months of obligated service remaining upon
completion of the course.  Rank requirement is waiverable to lance corporal
by course director/chief.
(5) Corporals to sergeants will complete the Ground Ordnance
Maintenance Supervisors Course (A14GBC6) and must remain on active duty for
12 months upon completion of the course.
(6) Staff Sergeants to gunnery sergeants will complete the Ground
Ordnance Managers Course (A14GBS6) and must remain on active duty for 12
months upon completion of the course.
(7) Marines completing drill instructor, recruiting duty, etc. are
recommended to attend the intermediate course prior to MARFOR assignment
(refresher training is not TECOM funded).
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness Manual.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Bus and Truck Mechanics and Diesel Engine Specialists 49-3031.
f.  Related Military Skill.  Engineer Equipment Mechanic, 1341.
-  MOS 2143, Amphibious Combat Vehicle (ACV) Technician (GySgt to Pvt) PMOS,
## DC I&L #
a.  Summary.  The ACV Technician performs basic automotive duties
including the inspection, maintenance, and repair of the ACV family of
vehicles.  The ACV Technician performs basic shop administrative actions
utilizing technical manuals and automated information systems.  The ACV
Technician can also operate the ACV FOV in support of combat and/or garrison
operations.  The ACV Technician will perform operator and field-level
maintenance and maintain/manage associated tools and equipment, of which
licensed to operate.  Accordingly, they will employ land navigation
techniques and must be knowledgeable with the operation of radios and weapon
systems when mounted on the vehicle during tactical operations.  The Gunnery
Sergeant to corporal level supervises, and performs various duties incident
to the inspection, maintenance, and repair of the automotive and RWS/Turret
systems of the ACV.  In addition, ACV Technicians prepare, maintain, and
supervise shop maintenance and management programs, and coordinate
maintenance and recovery operations according to grade and position.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess an MM score of 105 or higher.
(2) Must have WS-B water survival qualification.
(3) Must have normal vision to include color, acuity, field of vision
and depth perception.  The examinee will not be disqualified for a vehicle
operator license because of color blindness or depth perception.  However, if
color blindness or depth perception is indicated, he or she will be given

## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-144 Enclosure (1)
additional training, which will enable him or her to drive safely.  Must have
normal color vision.
(4) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. xiv) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
(5) Must meet MOS classification standards as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(6) No more than one yes in the blocks contained in section 6 on the
physical fitness inquiry for motor vehicle operators form (OF-345) is
acceptable.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Amphibious Combat Vehicle Basic Repairer Course
(M10AH33), Camp Pendleton, CA.
(2) Complete the Amphibious Combat Vehicle Field Level Maintenance
New Equipment Training 2.0, licensed by the program manager.
(3) Entry level and lateral move Marines must complete the Amphibious
Combat Vehicle Technician Course for assignment as a primary MOS.
(4) Must complete the submerged vehicle egress trainer (SVET)
qualifications prior to completion of the Amphibious Combat Vehicle
## Technician Course.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference, NAVMC 3500.2D.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Bus and Truck Mechanics and Diesel Engine Specialists 49-3031.
f.  Related Military Skill.  Engineer Equipment Mechanic, 1341.
# For Marines that are trained on an amphibious combat vehicle in the legacy
field level maintenance new equipment training are required to complete the
evaluation program signed off on by the officer in charge of the training
transition unit in order to be awarded the 2143 PMOS.
-  MOS 2146, Heavy Ordnance Vehicle Repairer/Technician (GySgt to Pvt) PMOS,
## DC I&L
a.  Summary.  THIS IS AN OBSOLETE MOS, NO LONGER IN USE IN THE TABLE OF
## ORGANIZATION OF THE MARINE CORPS.  THIS MOS IS MAINTAINED IN THE MOS MANUAL
BECAUSE IT APPEARS IN THE RECORDS OF MARINES STILL ON ACTIVE DUTY.  The Heavy
Ordnance Vehicle (HOV) Repairer/Technician performs basic automotive duties
including the inspection, maintenance, and repair of the marine corps combat
tank (M1A1), recovery vehicle (M88A2), armored vehicle launcher bridge
(AVLB), and the assault breacher vehicle (AVB).  The HOV Repairer/Technician
performs basic shop administrative actions utilizing technical manuals and
automated information systems.  The HOV Repairer/Technician also operates the
M88A2 recovery vehicle in support of combat and/or garrison operations.  As a
member of the recovery crew, the HOV Repairer/Technician will perform
operator and field-level maintenance and maintain/manage associated tools and
equipment, of which licensed to operate.  Accordingly, they will employ land

## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-145 Enclosure (1)
navigation techniques and must be knowledgeable with the operation of radios
and weapon systems when mounted on the recovery vehicle during tactical
operations.  At the grades of sergeant and above, the HOV Repairer/Technician
trains, supervises, and performs various duties and tasks related to the
inspection, maintenance, and repair of the automotive and turret systems of
the marine corps M1A1, M88A2, AVLB, and the ABV.  In addition, HOV Repairer/
Technicians prepare, maintain, and supervise shop maintenance and management
programs, and coordinate maintenance and recovery operations according to
grade and position.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must possess an GT score of 100.
(2) Must possess an EL score of 105.
(3) Must possess an MM score of 105 or higher.
(4) Must meet MOS classification standards, as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(5) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. xiv) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
(6) No more than one yes in the blocks contained in section 6 on the
physical fitness inquiry for motor vehicle operators form (OF-345) is
acceptable.
(7) Must have normal vision to include color, acuity, field of vision
and depth perception.  The examinee will not be disqualified for a vehicle
operator’s license because of color blindness or depth perception.  However,
if color blindness or depth perception is indicated, he or she will be given
additional training, which will enable him or her to drive safely.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the M1A1 Tank System Mechanic Course (A03GBN2), Ft
Moore, GA.
(2) Sergeants through gunnery sergeants will complete the Ground
Ordnance Managers Course (A14GBS6) and must remain on active duty for 12
months upon completion of the course.
(3) Corporals to staff sergeants are expected to complete the M1A1
Main Battle Tank Technician Course (A03GBQ2) and have 12 months or more on
active duty upon completion of the course.
(4) Must possess the qualifications required for licensing as an
Ordnance Vehicle Operator per MCO 8400.6.
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Bus and Truck Mechanics and Diesel Engine Specialists 49-3031.


## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-146 Enclosure (1)
f.  Related Military Skill
## (1) Engineer Equipment Mechanic, 1341.
## (2) Towed Artillery Systems Technician, 2131.
-  MOS 2147, Light Armored Vehicle (LAV) Repairer/Technician (GySgt to Pvt)
## PMOS, DC I&L
a.  Summary.  The LAV Repairer/Technician performs basic automotive
duties including the inspection, maintenance, and repair of the LAV family of
vehicles.  The LAV Repairer/Technician performs basic shop administrative
actions utilizing technical manuals and automated information systems.  The
LAV Repairer/Technician also operates the LAV recovery vehicle in support of
combat and/or garrison operations.  As a member of the recovery crew, the LAV
Repairer/Technician will perform operator and field-level maintenance and
maintain/manage associated tools and equipment, of which licensed to operate.
Accordingly, they will employ land navigation techniques and must be
knowledgeable with the operation of radios and weapon systems when mounted on
the vehicle during tactical operations.  At the grade of corporal and above,
the LAV Technician trains, supervises, and performs various duties related to
the inspection, maintenance, and repair of automotive and turret systems of
the LAV family of vehicles.  In addition, LAV Technicians prepare, maintain,
and supervise shop maintenance and management programs, and coordinate
maintenance and recovery operations according to grade and position.
b.  Prerequisites
(1) Must pass an audiogram hearing test.
(2) Must possess an MM score of 105 or higher.  This prerequisite is
non-waiverable.
(3) Must meet MOS classification standards as listed in the glossary
(pg. xiv) prior to graduating from recruit training.
(4) Must meet initial strength test standards associated with PEF
assignments as listed in the glossary (pg. xiv) prior to shipping to recruit
training.
(5) No more than one yes in the blocks contained in section 6 on the
physical fitness inquiry for motor vehicle operators form (OF-345) is
acceptable.
(6) Must possess a valid state driver's license.  Past driving
record, evidenced by a search of a motor vehicle records check, reveals no
active state driver's license suspensions or revocations.
(7) Must have normal vision to include color, acuity, field of vision
and depth perception.  The examinee will not be disqualified for a vehicle
operator’s license because of color blindness or depth perception.  However,
if color blindness or depth perception is indicated, he or she will be given
additional training, which will enable him or her to drive safely.
c.  Requirements
(1) Complete the Light Armored Vehicle Repair Marine Course
## (A14GBD6).

## 3120 NAVMC 1200.1L



## 3-147 Enclosure (1)
(2) Must possess the qualifications required for licensing as an
Ordnance Vehicle Operator per MCO 8400.6.
(3) In order to remain fit for duty, must have a current WSI swim
qualification.
(4) Corporals through gunnery sergeants are expected to have
completed the Light Armored Vehicles Technician Course (A14GBH6); and have a
minimum of 12 months of obligated service remaining upon completion of the
course.  Rank requirement is waiverable to lance corporal by course director/
chief.
(5) Corporals to sergeants will complete the Ground Ordnance
Maintenance Supervisors Course (A14GBC6) and must remain on active duty for
12 months upon completion of the course.
(6) Staff Sergeants to gunnery sergeants will complete the Ground
Ordnance Managers Course (A14GBS6) and must remain on active duty for 12
months upon completion of the course.
(7) Marines completing drill instructor, recruiting duty, etc. are
recommended to attend the intermediate course prior to MARFOR assignment
(refresher training is not TECOM funded).
d.  Duties.  For a complete listing of duties and tasks, refer to
reference (au), Ground Ordnance Maintenance Training and Readiness.
e.  Related Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) Title and Code.
Automotive Service Technicians and Mechanics 49-3023.
f.  Related Military Skill.  Engineer Equipment Mechanic, 1341.
-  MOS 2149, Ordnance Vehicle Maintenance Chief (MGySgt to MSgt) PMOS, DC
## I&L
a.  Summary.  Ground Ordnance Vehicle Maintenance Chiefs supervise the
maintenance, repair, and inspection of ground ordnance vehicles and support
equipment.  Additionally, Ordnance Vehicle Maintenance Chiefs supervise and
manage the administration of ordnance vehicle related maintenance programs
and facilities.  The Ordnance Vehicle Maintenance Chief is technically
proficient in the following areas organizational, maintenance, and resource
management, specifically in the areas of training, records and reporting via
major automated information systems, preventive and corrective maintenance,
equipment availability, publications control, supply support, and the
oversight of command environmental and hazardous waste programs.  The Ground
